questions.explanation: Phloem fibres are thick walled, elongated spindle shaped dead cells which possess narrow lumen. They provide mechanical support to the tissue. Phloem parenchyma are thin walled-living cells of parenchyma. They have two functions, storage and lateral food conduction.

questions.question: Which of the following does not loss their nucleus at maturity?
questions.explanation: Companion cells are present along the sieve tube, connected to them via plasmodesmata. These cells are metabolically active and sieve tube elements are dependent on these cells they do not lose nucleus at maturity. RBC vessels and sieve tube cells lose their nucleus at maturity.

questions.question: In desert plants, rate of water loss gets reduced due to the presence of
questions.explanation: Cuticle are protective, hydrophobic waxy covering produced by epidermal cells of leaves, young shoots and other aerial parts. It minimises the water loss through transpiration (with the help of stomata) and also reduces pathogen entry.

questions.question: A long tree has several branches. The tissue that helps in the side ways conduction of water in the branches is
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels are very long tube-like structures formed by a row of cells placed end to end. The transverse walls between these cells are partially or completely dissolved to form continuous water channels.

questions.question: If the tip of sugarcane plant is removed from the field, even then it keeps on growing in length. It is due to the presence of
questions.explanation: If the tip of sugarcane plant is removed the apical meristem is also removed as it is situated in the apices of growing roots and stem. Intercalary meristem are located at the base of leaves or nodes and leads to the increase in the length of an organ such as leaves and internodes.

questions.question: A nail is inserted in the trunk of a tree at a height of 1 metre from the ground level. After 3 years the nail will
questions.explanation: When a nail is inserted in the trunk of a tree at a height of 1 metre from the ground, even after 3 year the nail remains at same level. It does not moves upwards as the apical meristem responsible for growth (length) is present in the apices only and lateral meristem responsible of increase in girth will lead to no change in length.

questions.question: Parenchyma cells are
questions.explanation: Parenchyma cells form the bulk of the plant body. Its cells are living and they possess the power of division. The cells are rounded or isodiametric, i.e., equally expanded on all sides. The cells are oval, round, polygonal or elongated in shape with a thin cell wall. It encloses a dense cytoplasm, which contains small nucleus and surrounds large central vacoule.

questions.question: Flexibility in plants is due to
questions.explanation: Collenchyma consist of living cells and are characterised by the presence of cellulose. Collenchyma is a mechanical tissue in young dicotyledonous stems and provides mechanical support and elasticity. It provides great tensile strength with flexibility to those organs in which it is found. It allows easy bending in various parts of a plant mainly young growing stem without breaking them.

questions.question: Cork cells are made impervious to water and gases by the presence of
questions.explanation: The walls of cork cells are heavily thickened with an organic substance, suberin. Suberin makes these cells impervious to water and gases.

questions.question: Survival of plants in terrestrial environment has been made possible by the presence of
questions.explanation: The conducting tissues in plants conduct different saps and have different structures. The primary conducting tissues of plants are xylem and phloem. Xylem conducts water from roots to the other parts of the plant, whereas phloem transports food and other material from the leaves to other parts of plants.

questions.question: The water conducting tissue generally present in gymnosperm is
questions.explanation: The gymnosperms are characterised by the presence of tracheids as their major conducting tissue. These are elongated dead cells with hard lignified walls. They conduct water and do not have open ends like the vessels.

questions.question: The number of cellular layers keeps on changing in
questions.explanation: The stratified epithelium contains more than one layer of cells i.e. 2 to 20 or more layers. Skin is an example of stratified epithelium. Stratification of layers prevents wear and tear. The number of cellular layers keeps on changing in the stratified epithelium.

questions.question: In the stratified epithelium, the basement membrane is in contact with

options.option: the second layer of cells from the top
the second layer of cells from base

questions.explanation: Stratified epithelium contains two or more layers of epithelial cells with the cells in the deepest (basal) layer in contact with the basement membrane. The multiple cell layers make it strong and capable of resisting stress and protecting underlying tissue.

questions.question: Actively diving cells are present in
questions.explanation: Cambium in plants is a layer of actively dividing cells between xylem (wood) and phloem (bast) tissues that are responsible for the secondary growth of stems and roots.

questions.question: A permanent tissue is described as complex tissue when it has

options.option: more than one type of cells, performing different functions
more than one type of cells, performing one common function
one type of cells, performing two or more different functions
one type of cells, performing one function

questions.explanation: The complex tissue consists of more than one type of cell. All the cells coordinate to perform a common function. they transport water, minerals salts, food material to various parts of the plant body.

questions.question: Smooth muscle consists of __________ filaments that are not arranged into sarcomeres giving it a non-striated pattern.
questions.explanation: Smooth muscle consists of thick and thin filaments that are not arranged into sarcomeres giving it a non-striated pattern.

questions.question: ________ connects two bones at a joint.
questions.explanation: A bone is connected with another bone by a connective tissue called ligaments. A bone is connected by muscle with another connective tissue called a tendon.

questions.question: ________ in the cell wall of cork/bark makes it impervious to water.
questions.explanation: The cork is impervious to water due to suberin deposition in the cell wall of its cells. The walls of cork cells are heavily thickened with an organic substance, suberin. Suberin makes these cells impervious to water and gases.

questions.question: The simple permanent tissue that consists of thick-walled, narrow, elongated, dead cells is ________.
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma Cells are long, thick-walled, and lignified with tapering ends. These are fiber-like in appearance and also known as sclerenchymatous fiber. These are dead cells and perform a mechanical function.

questions.question: The common characteristic of xylem tracheids and sieve tubes is that both are:
questions.explanation: Xylem is composed of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma, and xylem fibres. Tracheids and vessels are tubular structures and thus they provide a channel for conduction of water and minerals. Phloem is composed of sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibre and phloem parenchyma. Sieve tubes are tubular cells with perforated walls. Sieve tubes are the conducting elements of phloem.

questions.question: We use glycerine in temporary mount of the material because:

options.option: it increases the clarity of the material
it provides the medium for floating the material

questions.explanation: Glycerine is used for temporary mounting as it is hygroscopic and does not allow the mounted material to dry up.

questions.question: Which of the following components are seen in a slide of human cheek cell when observed under a microscope?
questions.explanation: The cells do not have a cell wall. However, each cell has a thin cell membrane. A large vacuole is present at the center of each cell and is surrounded by the cytoplasm. A lightly stained cytoplasm is observed in each cell. A deeply stained nucleus is observed at the center of each cell.

questions.question: Roshini is making a temporary mount of onion peel. What precautions should be taken to avoid the entry of air bubbles in the slide?

options.option: Coverslip should be gently dropped over the peel
Peel should be allowed to fold over itself once or twice
Coverslip should be dropped on to the peel from a certain height
Peel should be immersed in stain for over an hour

questions.explanation:
Precautions:

  1. The staining of the peel must be appropriate. Excess stain can be removed by rinsing the peel with water taken in the watch glass.
  2. Always hold the slide by its edges to avoid making the slide dirty.
  3. Use a brush to transfer the peel to the slide.
  4. The peel should never be folded.
  5. The peel should be mounted in the centre of the slide.
  6. Always keep the coverslip gently to avoid the entry of air bubbles.

questions.question: The extra stain from the slide if present is removed by:
questions.explanation: The staining of the peel must be appropriate. Excess stain can be removed by rinsing the peel with water taken in the watch glass. The extra stain from the slide if present is removed by soaking with filter paper. Filter paper absorbs the excess water and stain.

questions.question: To prepare a mount of human cheek cell, the sample is collected from:

options.option: outer side of cheek with a toothpick
inner side of cheek with a blade
outer side of cheek with a blade
inner side of cheek with a toothpick

questions.explanation: While preparing a mount of human cheek cell, the sample is collected from the inner side of the cheek using a toothpick, which will collect some cheek cells.

questions.question: A student used a red stain for mounting a peel of onion. This corresponds to the stain:
questions.explanation:
A student used a red stain for mounting a peel of onion This corresponds to the stain Safranin.

  1. It is used in histology and cytology.
  2. The formula of Safranin is  C20H19N4+Cl-
  3. The molar mass of Safranin is 350.85 g·mol⁻¹
  4. It is soluble in water.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not visible in the cheek cells?
questions.explanation: The cells do not have a cell wall. However, each cell has a thin cell membrane. A large vacuole is present at the center of each cell and is surrounded by the cytoplasm. A lightly stained cytoplasm is observed in each cell. A deeply stained nucleus is observed at the center of each cell.

questions.question: On observing onion peel slide under low power of compound microscope which set of structures are clearly seen.
questions.explanation:
1. There are a large number of regularly shaped cells lying side by side and each cell has a distinct cell wall.
  2. A distinct nucleus is present on the periphery of each cell.
  3. A lightly stained cytoplasm is observed in each cell.
  4. A large vacuole is present at the center of each cell and is surrounded by the cytoplasm.

questions.question: You are viewing a prepared slide of striated muscle fibres from a cockroach leg. When you focus the microscope, the striations appear pale and indistinct. To make the stations clearly visible, you would:

options.option: remove the mirror to cut out light
replace the objective to decrease the magnification
slowly close the diaphragm to reduce the light

questions.explanation: To make the stations clearly visible slowly close the diaphragm to reduce the light. The reduction of light gives a better contrast.

questions.question: Which among the following helps to increase the diameter or girth of plant organs like stem?
questions.explanation: Lateral meristem is responsible for increasing the girth or circumference of the root or stem in a plant. It is found parallel to the long axis in a plant body. The secondary growth in this meristem enhances the girth more than the overall length of the plant. Apical meristem is responsible for the increase of girth of the root.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is the best description of the composition of blood?
questions.explanation:
Blood is classified as connective tissue and consists of two main components:

  1. Plasma, which is a clear extracellular fluid.
  2. Formed elements, which are made up of the blood cells and platelets.

Formed elements are:

  • Erythrocytes, also known as red blood cells (RBCs)
  • Leukocytes, also known as white blood cells (WBCs)
  • Platelets

questions.question: The smooth muscle consists of _______________. Each fibre contains a single oval nucleus in its thick middle part. The cross-striations are absent so that the fibres look smooth, hence the name unstriated.
questions.explanation: The smooth muscle consists of long, narrow unbranched spindle-shaped fibres. Each fibre contains a single oval nucleus in its thick middle part. The cross-striations are absent so that the fibres look smooth, hence the name unstriated.

questions.question: Name the muscle which is found in visceral organs.
questions.explanation: Visceral muscle tissue, or smooth muscle, is tissue associated with the internal organs of the body, especially those in the abdominal cavity.

questions.question: The efferent part of neuron is _____
questions.explanation: In the peripheral nervous system, an efferent nerve fiber is the axon of a motor neuron.

questions.question: Tongue is made up of _____.
questions.explanation: The tongue is under voluntary control via the somatic nervous system, therefore it is made up of skeletal muscle.  Skeletal muscle is found in other places as well - not just attached to the bones.

questions.question: Which cell does not have perforated cell wall?
questions.explanation: Tracheids and vessels are xylem elements and are concerned with the transport of water. They are long tube-like structures with partially or completely dissolved walls to form water pipes (in vessels) and pits in cell wall (in tracheids) for conducting water. Sieve tubes are slender tube-like structures with their end walls perforated by numerous pores and are called sieve plates. They are phloem elements and are main food conducting elements. Companion cells possess numerous mitochondria and ribosomes and are supporting units of sieve tubes.

questions.question: The end of a long bone is connected to another bone by
questions.explanation: Two bones can be connected to each other by another type of connective tissue called the ligament.

questions.question: Cambium is an example of
questions.explanation: Lateral meristem is present along the side of the stem, vascular cambium in the gymnosperms is a good example of the lateral meristem.

questions.question: Ligaments and Tendons are formed of
questions.explanation: Two bones can be connected to each other by another type of connective tissue called the ligament. Tendons connect bones to muscles and are another type of connective tissue.

questions.question: One of the following is not a characteristic feature of parenchyma tissue. That feature is:

options.option: a large single vacuole is present in each cell
large cells are placed together with intercellular spaces

questions.explanation:
Intercellular spaces are present between the cells. Cells are thin-walled (primary wall only except wood parenchyma), with large vacuole and wide intercellular spaces. Cells of Parenchyma tissue are isodiametric.
Cells are thick at the corners is not a characteristic feature of parenchyma tissue.

questions.question: While preparing a temporary mount of cheek cells, teacher asked Rohit to pick up the stain. He had four bottles A, B, C, and D containing methylene blue, glycerine, distilled water, Canada balsum. Which one should he pick?
questions.explanation: Methylene blue is used to stain human cheek epithelial cells better. Methylene blue stains negatively charged molecules in the cell, including DNA and RNA. This dye is toxic when ingested and it causes irritation when in contact with the skin and eyes.

questions.question: Choose the chemical compound with which the specimen is temporarily mounted.
questions.explanation: Glycerine is a good dehydrating agent. It avoids the drying of the specimen. Besides, glycerine tends to reflect light due to its refractive nature. As a result of it, the image appears clearer under the microscope. Due to these reasons, glycerine is used while preparing a temporary mount of leaf peel.

questions.question: The cell appear elongated, tapering at ends as observed under a microscope. It is
questions.explanation:
There are two types of sclerenchyma cells:

  1. fibres which are elongated cells with tapering ends and
  2. sclereids (also called stone cells), which are roughly isodiametric cells with narrow cavities.

questions.question: Which of the following tissues divide continuously and are found on growing regions of the plants?
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissues divide continuously and are found in growing regions of the plants. Thus they are responsible for increasing the length and girth of the plant.

questions.question: Parenchyma which contains chlorophyll and helps in photosynthesis is called ________.
questions.explanation: Parenchyma tissues which contain chlorophyll and helps in photosynthesis are called chlorenchyma.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a function of the epidermis?
questions.explanation: The epidermis does not conduct the food to the various parts of the plant. Conduction of food is carried by phloem tissue.

questions.question: Girth of stem increases due to
questions.explanation: The lateral meristematic tissues are responsible for an increase in the diameter or girth of the plant.

questions.question:
Which muscles act involuntarily?

  1. Striated muscles
  2. Smooth muscles
  3. Cardiac muscles
  4. Skeletal muslces
questions.explanation: The muscles which are not under the control of our will, are called involuntary muscles. Smooth (unstriated) muscles and cardiac muscles are involuntary muscles.

questions.question: Which connective tissue supports and provides flexibility to the body parts?
questions.explanation: Cartilage is a connective tissue that provides support and flexibility to the body parts. It smoothens bone surfaces at joints. It is also present in the nose, ear, trachea, and larynx.

questions.question: Which is the most widely distributed connective tissue?
questions.explanation: Areolar connective tissue is the simplest and most widely distributed connective tissue. Areolar connective tissue is found between the skin and muscles, around blood vessels and nerves, and in the bone marrow.

questions.question: The walls of cork cells are thickened by the deposition of an organic substance which make these cells impermeable to water and gases. This substance is
questions.explanation: Cork cells are dead cells without intercellular spaces. Their cell wall is very thick due to the deposition of an organic substance called suberin, which is impermeable to water and gases.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding apical meristem is incorrect?

options.option: It develops by dedifferentiation of permanent tissues.
It brings about increase in length of the root and stem.

questions.explanation: Apical meristem is present at the root, shoots apex and is always a meristematic tissue and not dedifferentiated and is responsible for primary growth.

questions.question:
Select the correct statement regarding meristematic tissues.

  1. They are made up of mature cells and are fully differentiated.
  2. They have cells with thick secondary walls.
  3. They perform the function of growth and division.
  4. Both they are made up of mature cells and are fully differentiated and they perform the function of growth and division.
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissues are plant tissues found at specific locations of the plant body and performs the function of growth and division. They are simple tissues composed of a group of similar and immature cells which divide to form new cells. They have cells with thin and elastic primary walls and abundant cytoplasm.

questions.question: X is a living tissue that provides mechanical support, tensile strength and elasticity in young dicotyledonous stem. X is ________.
questions.explanation: Collenchyma is a living tissue that provides mechanical support, tensile strength and elasticity in young dicotyledonous stem.

questions.question: The tissue shown here is ________.
questions.explanation: The figure shown here is of adipose tissue. It is primarily a fat storing tissue in which the matrix is packed with adipocytes. The tissue is found beneath the skin, in the covering of heart, around the blood vessels and kidneys, etc.

questions.question: Which of the following plant tissues provide mechanical support and a protective covering around seeds?
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma consists of thick walled dead cells and their cell walls are thickened due to the deposition of a water resistant material lignin. Main function of sclerenchyma is to provide mechanical support to plant parts and it also provides protective covering around seeds.

questions.question: Which of the following epithelial tissues, lines the stomach and intestine?
questions.explanation: Columnar epithelium consists of pillar-like cells that are much taller than wide. It lines the stomach, intestine, etc.

questions.question:
Which of the following are correctly matched?

  1. Dense regular - Blubber connective tissue
  2. Cartilage - Pinnae of ear
  3. Areolar tissue - Packaging tissue
  4. Cuboidal epithelium - Kidney tubules
  5. Cardiac muscle - Branched and multinucleate
  6. Striated muscle - Unbranched and uninucleate
questions.explanation: Blubber of whales is a type of adipose tissue. Adipose tissue is a type of loose connective tissue. Cardiac muscles are present in the wall of heart. They are uninucleate and branched whereas striated muscles are attached to the bones, they are multinucleate and unbranched.

questions.question: Pankaj was asked to draw a labelled diagram of scierenchyma tissue as seen in longitudinal section. The figure he drew is shown here. Point out the mistake in the figure.

options.option: Instead of simple pit pore, it should be bordered pit.
The cell walls are not lignified in scierenchyma.
The nucleus and cytoplasm are not shown.
The figure shows transverse section instead of longitudinal section of tissue.

questions.explanation: The figure shows transverse section of sclerenchyma tissue. It is a simple supportive tissue of highly thick-walled cells with little or no cytoplasm. The thickening of cell walls may be due to deposition of lignin or both lignin and cellulose. The adjacent cells possess simple oblique pits.

questions.question: The tissue shown in the given figure is

options.option: Scierenchyma, in which uniform wall thickenings are present
Angular collenchyma, in which wall thickenings are present at the angles
Xylem vessel that forms long channels for conduction of water and minerals

questions.explanation: Figure shows Xylem vessel that forms long channels for conduction of water and minerals

questions.question:
Match column I with column II and select the correct option from the given codes.
 

Column I Column II
(a)  (i) Phagocytosis
(b)  (ii) Release heparin
(c)  (iii) Allergic response
questions.explanation: The given figures (a), (b) and (c) represent eosinophil, monocyte and basophil respectively.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct about the cell shown in figure?

options.option: P conducts impulse away from cyton while S conducts impulse towards cyton.
P receives nerve impulse from Q and conveys it to other cells.

questions.explanation: In the given figure, ‘P represent dendrites, ‘Q’ is neurilemma, ‘R’ is node of Ranvier and ‘S’ is axon ending. The dendrites receive impulses while axon takes impulses away from the cell body or cyton.

questions.question:
Select the incorrect differences between collenchyma and scierenchyma.

  Collenchyma Scierenchyma
(i) It is made up of dead cells. Its cells are living
(ii) Lumen or cell cavity is wide. Lumen or cell cavity is usually narrow.
(iii) It provides hardness to the region where it occurs. It keeps the organ soft.
(iv) Wall thickening is not uniform. Wall thickening is uniform.
(v) Pits are simple and straight Pits are simple, oblique and may be branched.
questions.explanation: In plants, collenchyma is a permanent living tissue of primary body. It provides flexibility to soft aerial parts like leaves, young stem of plant so that they can bend without breaking. Sclerenchyma consists of thick-walled, dead cells. It provides hardness to the region where it occurs.

questions.question: Observe the given figures of three different plant tissues and select the correct statement.

options.option: Cytoplasm is present in cells of a while it is absent in cells of b and c.
a provides mechanical strength to the plant while b and c serve as storage tissues.
Cell walls of a, b and c bear uniform thickening of suberin.
a and b consist of living cells while c consists of dead cells.

questions.explanation: In the given figure of plant tissues ‘a’ and ‘b’ represent parenchymatous and collenchymatous tissues respectively that are living tissues whereas figure ‘c’ represents sclerenchymatous tissue which comprises of dead cells.

questions.question:
Match column I with column II and select the correct option from the given codes.

Column I Column II
(a) Food storage (i) Phloem
(b) Food conduction (ii) Xylem
(c) Mechanical strength (iii) Parenchyma
(d) Water conduction (iv) Sclerenchyma
questions.explanation:
(a) - (iii), (b) - (i), (c) - (iv), (d) - (ii)

Column I Column II
(a) Food storage Parenchyma
(b) Food conduction Phloem
(c) Mechanical strength Sclerenchyma
(d) Water conduction Xylem

questions.question: The numerical ratio of displacement to distance for a moving object is
questions.explanation:
We know, the magnitude of displacement is the straight line distance between the initial point and the final point of the path of the moving body.
Displacement could be negative, positive, or zero. but the distance is always positive or zero when there is no motion.
So, the ratio of displacement to distance for a moving object is equal to less than 1.

questions.question: What is the slope of the body when it moves with uniform velocity?
questions.explanation: The slope is zero (because the slope for the velocity time graph gives the acceleration).

questions.question: Which of the following is the position-time graph for a body at rest?
questions.explanation:
The above graph shows an object which is not moving i.e. it is at rest.
The straight horizontal line parallel to the time axis shows that the distance covered by the body remains constant with the change in time.

questions.question: What does the area of the velocity-time graph give?
questions.explanation:
The area enclosed by the velocity-time graph and time axis gives the magnitude of displacement.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} velocity = {tex} \frac{displacement}{Time}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Velocity {tex}\times{/tex} time = displacement

questions.question: If a body starts from rest, what can be said about the acceleration of the body?
questions.explanation: If a body starts from rest, it starts moving. that means the change in velocity is positive. That means there is a POSITIVE acceleration.

questions.question: What does the slope of the position-time graph indicate?
questions.explanation: The slope of a position graph represents the velocity of the object. The steeper the slope is, the faster the motion is changing. So the value of the slope at a particular time represents the velocity of the object at that instant. Average velocity can be calculated from a position-time graph as the change in position divided by the corresponding change in time.

questions.question: When a body moves uniformly along the circle, then:-

options.option: its velocity changes but speed remain the same
its speed changes but velocity remain the same
both speed and velocity remain the same.

questions.explanation: Speed remains the same as it is 'uniform' circular motion and velocity changes at every point because of direction changes. The tendency of the object at every point is to go tangential to its position.

questions.question: What does the slope of the velocity-time graph give?
questions.explanation:
We can find out the value of acceleration from the slope of the velocity-time graph of a moving body.
Acceleration = {tex}Change\,\,in\,\,velocity\over\, time{/tex} = Slope of the velocity-time graph provided.

questions.question: The displacement of the body can be-
questions.explanation: Displacement is the shortest distance travelled so it can be positive or negative or zero.

questions.question: Which of the following gives both direction and magnitude?
questions.explanation: Any quantity that needs to be fully described by identifying its magnitude and direction is referred to as a vector quantity. By magnitude, we mean the size of the quantity, such as length or strength. By direction, we mean where the vector is pointing or where it is being directed, such as left or right, north, south, east, or west, or even up or down.

questions.question: If a moving body comes to rest, then it's acceleration is:
questions.explanation: If a moving body comes to rest, then its acceleration is negative, as it’s velocity becomes zero. therefore the acceleration is also zero.

questions.question: A body is said to be in rest when:

options.option: The body moves in uniform motion, w.r.t observer.
It's position changes with time w.r.t observer.
Its position doesn’t change with time with respect to the observer.

questions.explanation: When a body does not change its position with respect to time, it is said that body is in a state of rest. Rest and motion are relative to the observer.

questions.question: A person sitting in the truck projected a ball vertically upwards. The ball:
questions.explanation: A person sitting in the truck projected a ball vertically upwards. The ball will fall back in his hand as the ball and the truck are moving at the same speed.

questions.question: Which of the following is the characteristic of distance travelled by an object?

options.option: It has a magnitude as well as specific direction
It has only magnitude and no specific direction
The distance travelled by an object is less than the magnitude of the displacement of the object.

questions.explanation: Distance is the length of path covered by a moving object in the given time irrespective of direction. Distance has only magnitude and no direction.

questions.question: Which of the following is the characteristic of displacement of an object?

options.option: The magnitude of the displacement is greater than the distance travelled by a moving object
Displacement has magnitude as well as specific direction
Displacement has only magnitude and no specific direction

questions.explanation: The shortest distance between the initial point and the final point is called displacement. Displacement has both magnitude and direction while distance has only magnitude.

questions.question: The displacement of a body is proportional to the cube of the time lapsed. The magnitude of the acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Acceleration is directly proportioned to time, it varies linearly with time and increases with respect to time.

questions.question: A body moves on three-quarters of a circle of radius r. The displacement and distance travelled by it
questions.explanation:
Distance covered is {tex}\frac34{/tex} circumference. 
{tex}\frac34\times2\times\mathrm\pi\times\mathrm r\;=\frac{3\mathrm{r}}2{/tex}
Displacement = Shortest Path

If We Join Point A and B we get right angle triangle with
Hypotenuse(displacement) and other two sides of a triangle with sides r.
Displacement = {tex}\sqrt{r^2+\;r^2}\;=\sqrt{2r^2}\;=\sqrt2r{/tex}

questions.question: A body moving with uniform acceleration has velocities 20 ms-1 and 30 ms-1. when passing two points A and B. Then the velocity midway between A and B is:
questions.explanation:
Let the acceleration of the car is = a
And the distance between A and B is = d
v2 - u2 = 2ad
v = 30m/s and u = 20m/s
2ad = 302 - 202
Ad = {tex}\frac{(900-400)}2{/tex} = 250 
When the car is at the mid point of AB then let the speed of the car is v1
v12 - 202 = 2a ({tex}\frac d2{/tex})
v12 = ad + 400 = 250 + 400 = 650
therefore v1 is = 25.4950m/s.

questions.question: A moving body is covering a distance directly proportional to the square of time. The acceleration of the body is:
questions.explanation:
Displacement is proportional to square of time
s is displacement and t is time then,
s {tex}\propto{/tex} t2
s = kt2
velocity(v) = 2kt
Acceleration(a) = 2k = constant.
So, the motion of the body is constant acceleration.

questions.question: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the value of:
questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph represents the distance covered and the gradient of a velocity-time graph represents the acceleration.

questions.question: Usha swims in a 90 m long pool. She covers 180 m in one minute going either way. The average velocity is:
questions.explanation: The average velocity for the entire swing would be zero because its final position and initial position are identical.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from 18 km/h to 36 km/h in 5 sec. The acceleration is
questions.explanation:
Given,
u = 18km/h = 18×1000 / 60×60 = 5m/s
v = 36km/h = 36×1000 / 60×60 = 10m/s
t = 5 sec
acceleration, a = ?
1st equation of motion,
v = u + at
10 = 5 + 5a
5a = 5
a = 5/5 = 1m/s

questions.question: A ball is thrown up with a velocity of 20 ms-1. What is the time of flight, neglecting air resistance?
questions.explanation:
Using kinematic equation, v = u + a{tex}\times{/tex}t, when ball reaches to top of its trajectory, it's velocity becomes zero. Ball is thrown against gravity of earth, therefore a = - g, u = 20 m/s, we can find time of ascend as
0 = 20 - 10 {tex}\times{/tex} t
20 = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} t
t = {tex}\frac{20}{10}{/tex} = 2 sec
Time of ascend is same as time of descend = 2 sec.
Time of flight = time of ascend + time of descend
Therefore time of flight = 4 sec.

questions.question: A car travels 10 m in 5 seconds, 20 m in the next 10 seconds, and 30 m in the last 10 seconds. The average speed of the motion is:
questions.explanation:
The average distance covered in unit time by a moving object is called average speed. The average speed is the ratio of total distance covered and total time taken.
Average speed = {tex}\frac{Total\;distan ce\;covered}{Total\;time\;taken}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{10+20+30}{5\;+\;10\;+10}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{60}{25}{/tex} = 2.4 ms-1

questions.question: A particle is moving in a circular path of radius r. The displacement after half a circle would be
questions.explanation:
Given, after half the circle, the particle will reach the diametrically opposite point i.e., from point A to point 6. And we know displacement is shortest path between initial and final point.
Displacement after half circle = AB=OA + OB [{tex}\because{/tex} Given, OA and OB = r]

= r + r = 2r
Hence, the displacement after half circle is 2r.

questions.question: A body is thrown vertically upwards with velocity u, the greatest height h to which it will rise is
questions.explanation:
If a body is thrown vertically upwards with initial velocity "u"
Maximum Height of body = {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}{/tex}
As  2gh = v² - u²
At maximum height final velocity of body is zero.
⇒ v = 0
So, 2gh = v² - u²
 2gh = -u²
 h= - {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}{/tex}
Distance can't be negative so,  h= {tex}\frac{u^2}{2g}.{/tex}

questions.question: Equation of motion can be used for a body having:
questions.explanation: Relation among velocity, distance, time, and acceleration is called equations of motion. There are three equations of motion. Equation of motion can be used for a body having uniform acceleration.

questions.question: For a uniformly accelerated body with initial and final velocities as u and v ms-1, the average velocity is:
questions.explanation: Average velocity is the ratio of total displacement or total distance travelled by a body in a given interval of time.

questions.question: The area below v-t graph is a measure of:
questions.explanation: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the distance (magnitude of displacement) which has the unit is metre (m).

questions.question: If the velocity of a body is reducing, it is said to have
questions.explanation: If there is a decrease in acceleration, it is called Retardation. This means the rate of decrease in velocity is called retardation or negative acceleration or deceleration.

questions.question: If the v-t graph is a straight line inclined to the time axis, then:
questions.explanation: The negative value of acceleration signifies deceleration or in other words, the velocity is decreasing.

questions.question: A body starting at a point, say A, reaches, say B, ahead in a straight line and returns back to A. Then there is:
questions.explanation: When the body comes back to its starting point, it has zero resultant displacements but covers a certain non-zero distance.

questions.question: If the displacement of an object is proportional to square of time, then the object moves with
questions.explanation:
From second equation of motion, s = ut + {tex}\frac12{/tex} at2
if object starts from rest i.e., initial velocity(u) = 0 and acceleration(a) in time (t)
Then, s= 0 × t + {tex}\frac12{/tex} at2
s = {tex}\frac12{/tex}at2
s α t2, if a = constant
So, the object with constant or uniform acceleration.

questions.question: Suppose a boy is enjoying a ride on a merry-go-round which is moving with a constant speed of 10ms-1. It implies that the boy is
questions.explanation: In a merry go round,the speed is constant but the velocity is not constant because its direction will be changing i.e, there is acceleration in the motion.So the boy is in accelerated motion.

questions.question: Area under a v - t graph represents a physical quantity which has the unit
questions.explanation: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the distance (magnitude of displacement) which has the unit: metre (m)

questions.question: Which of the following figures represent uniform motion of moving object correctly?
questions.explanation: Uniform motion of a moving object

questions.question: Slope of a velocity-time graph gives
questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph represents the distance covered and the gradient of a velocity-time graph represents the acceleration.

questions.question: In which of the following cases of motion, the distance moved and the magnitude of displacement are equal?

options.option: A car is moving in a circular path
A car is moving on a straight road
The earth is revolving around the Sun
The pendulum is moving to and fro

questions.explanation: The distance moved and magnitude of displacement are equal only in the case of motion along a straight line. Because displacement is the shortest path between initial and find path. So, for car moving on straight road, distance moved and magnitude of displacement are equal.

questions.question: A body moving in a circle of radius r covers {tex}\frac 34{/tex}th of the circle. The ratio of the distance to displacement is:
questions.explanation:
r' = {tex}r\sqrt2{/tex} displacement
distance = {tex}\frac{3\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}2{/tex}
ratio {tex}=\frac{\frac{3\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}}2}{r\sqrt2}{/tex}
ratio = {tex}3\pi{/tex} : {tex}2\sqrt 2 {/tex}

questions.question: A signal from a space ship reaches the ground in 5 minutes. What was the distance of the space ship from the ground station? The speed of the signal is 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s.
questions.explanation:
Time = 5 minutes = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 300 sec.
Distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s {tex}\times{/tex} 300 = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010 m

questions.question: Two cars A and B race each other. The Car A ran for 2 min at a speed of 7.5 km/h, slept for 56 min and again ran for 2 min at a speed of 7.5 km/h. The average speed of the car A in the race is:
questions.explanation:
Distance = speed {tex}\times{/tex} time
Distance travelled in first 2 min = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac2{60}{/tex} = 0.25 km
Distance travelled in last 2 min = 7.5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac2{60}{/tex}= 0.25 km
Total distance = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5 km
Total time = 2 + 2 + 56 = 60 min = 1 hr
Average speed = {tex}\frac{0.5}1{/tex}
= 0.5 km/hr

questions.question: The maximum speed of a train is 90 km/h. It takes 10 hours to cover a distance of 500 km. The ratio of its average speed to maximum speed is:
questions.explanation:
Average speed = {tex}\frac{500}{10}{/tex} = 50 km/hr
Ratio of average speed to maximum speed = 50 : 90 = 5:9

questions.question: A boy runs for 10 minutes at a uniform speed of 9 kmh-1. At what speed should he run for the next 20 minutes so that the average speed becomes 12 kmh-1?
questions.explanation:
Let, the boy runs with speed v for next 20 min
Given that, average speed = 12 km/h-1
As, average speed = {tex}\frac{\text { Total distance covered }}{\text { Total time taken }}{/tex}
12 = {tex}\frac{\text { Total distance covered }}{\frac{(10+20)}{60} \mathrm{~h}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Total distance covered = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {30}{60}{/tex} = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac 12{/tex} = 6 km
Distance covered in 10 min = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac {10}{60}{/tex} = 1.5 km
Remaining distance = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5 km
For next 20 min, he should run with speed
v = {tex}\frac{4.5}{\frac{20} {60}}{/tex} = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 13.5 km h-1

questions.question: A parachutist jumps off the plane and opens up the parachute at time t1. He eventually reaches a steady speed and finally hits the floor at time t2. Which of the following shows the speed-time graph of the parachutist’s descent?
questions.explanation:
When the parachutist jumps off the plane his vertical speed is 0 m/s. The speed will increase constantly due to the gravitational field strength of the Earth, which makes the parachutist accelerate uniformly downwards. At this point, choice B and choice D are eliminated because the two graphs show a deceleration.
When the parachute opens, there is a sudden strong opposing air resistance acting against the parachutist, which causes the speed of the parachutist to drop drastically and eventually reach a constant speed known as terminal velocity.

questions.question: A particle is moving in a straight line with initial velocity u and uniform acceleration a. If the sum of the distances travelled in tth and (t + 1)th seconds is 100 cm, then its velocity after t seconds in cm s-1 is
questions.explanation:
Distance travelled in tth second of uniformly accelerated motion is
St = u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t - 1) ...(i)
Distance travelled in (t + 1)th second can be written as
St+1 = u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}[2(t + 1) - 1]
or St+1 = u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t + 1) ...(ii)
st + st+1 = 100 cm (given)
u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t -1) + u +{tex}\frac a2{/tex}(2t + 1) = 100 [Using (i) and (ii)]
or 2u + 2at = 100 or u + at = 50;
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 50 cms-1

questions.question: Two identical balls are at rest side by side at the bottom of a hill. Sometime after ball A is kicked up the hill, ball B is given a kick up the hill. Ball A is headed downhill when it passes ball B headed up the hill. At the instant when ball A passes ball B, it has the same
questions.explanation: Position and acceleration of ball A is same as that of ball B at the instant when ball A passes ball B.

questions.question:
A dancer is demonstrating dance steps along a straight line. The position-time graph of the dancer is given here.

The average speed for the dance step depicted by CD is
questions.explanation:
Distance covered for dance step CD = 4 m
Time taken for CD is greater than 1 s but less than 2 s
Since, average speed ={tex}\frac{\text { total distance }}{\text { total time }}{/tex}
Therefore, average speed is less than 4 m s-1 but greater than 2 m s-1. Hence, option (C) is the only possibility.

questions.question:
A dancer is demonstrating dance steps along a straight line. The position-time graph of the dancer is given here.

The average velocity of the dancer during time interval t = 2 s to t = 9 s is
questions.explanation:
During time interval t = 2 s to t = 9 s, six dance steps namely AB, BC, CD, DE, EF and FG have been demonstrated by the dancer.
Total displacement = (0 - 2) = -2 m
Total time taken = 9 - 2 = 7s
Average velocity = {tex}\frac{\text { total displacement }}{\text { total time taken }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2}7{/tex} = -0.29 m s-1

questions.question: Figure shows the x-t plot of a particle moving along a straight line. Let v1 and v2 be average speeds in time intervals 1 and 2 respectively. Then
questions.explanation:
In time interval 1, magnitude of net displacement is more as compared to that time interval 2 i.e., {tex}\triangle{/tex}x1 > {tex}\triangle{/tex}x2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v1 > v2

questions.question:
Which of the following is a correct statement?

  1. Distance is the magnitude of displacement in all cases.
  2. When a body moves with uniform speed, then the average speed is same as instantaneous speed.
  3. Average speed is greater than the average velocity if a body is moving in a straight line without reversing its direction.
  4. When a body moves with constant velocity, the average velocity is zero.
questions.explanation: Statement (ii) is correct.

questions.question:
Match the column I with column II and mark the correct option from the following given codes.

Column I Column II
(a) 36 km h-1 (i) 20000 mm
(b) 1 m s-2 (ii) 980 cm s-2
(c) 9.8 m s-2 (iii) 12960 km h-2
(d) 0.02 km (iv) 10 m s-1
A ball is dropped onto the floor from a height of 20 m. It rebounds to a height of 10 m. If the ball is in contact with the floor for 0.1 seconds, what is the average acceleration during contact?
questions.explanation:
Here height from which ball is dropped,
h = 20 m
Using v2 = u2 + 2gh; v2 = 2gh ({tex}\because{/tex} u = 0)
or v ={tex}\sqrt{2 g h}{/tex} (downward)
Ball rebounds to a height, h' = 10 m
So, 02 = v2 - 2gh' or v' ={tex}\sqrt{2 g h'}{/tex} (upward)
Acceleration of the ball,
a = {tex}\frac{\left(v^{\prime}-v\right)}{t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left[\sqrt{2 g h^{\prime}}-(-\sqrt{2 g h})\right]}{t}{/tex}
or a = {tex}\frac{[\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 10}+\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 20}]}{0.1}{/tex} {tex}\simeq{/tex} 338 ms-2

questions.question: What would you conclude about the variation in velocity of a cyclist from the given graph?

options.option: Velocity changes non-linearly if acceleration is changing linearly.
Velocity changes linearly if acceleration is changing non-linearly.
Velocity becomes uniform if acceleration becomes infinite.
Velocity becomes zero if acceleration becomes zero.

questions.explanation: For the given graph, a = kt, where k is some positive constant. So, velocity v = u + at = u + (kt)t = 4 + kt2 i.e., varies non-linearly.

questions.question:
Statement 1: Reducing the initial velocity to half the initial value, the stopping distance of moving object decreases by a factor of 2 (for the same deceleration).
Statement 2: Stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity.

options.option: Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is false but statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation:
Let the distance travelled by the vehicle before it stops be ds, then substituting v = 0, x = ds (stopping distance) in v2 = u2 - 2ax, we get ds ={tex}\frac{u^2}{2 a}{/tex}.
Thus, the stopping distance is proportional to the square of the initial velocity.
So, if the initial velocity is reduced to half of its value, the stopping distance decreases by a factor of four.

questions.question: The water drop falls at regular intervals from a tap 5 m above the ground. The third drop is leaving the tap at instant the first drop touches the ground. How far above the ground is the second drop at that instant? (Take g = 10 m S-2)
questions.explanation:
Height of tap = 5 m
For the first drop, 5 = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}gt2 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex}10t2 = 5t2 or t2 = 1 or t = 1 s. It means that the third drop leaves after one second of the first drop, or each drop leaves after every 0.5 s. Distance covered by the second drop in 0.5 s
= {tex}\frac 12{/tex}gt2 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.5)2 = 1.25 m
Therefore distance of the second drop above the ground = 5 - 1.25 = 3.75 m.

questions.question:
A train moves from one station to another in two hours time. Its speed-time graph during the motion is shown in figure. Identify the correct statement(s) regarding this graph.

  1. The maximum acceleration during the journey is 120 km/h2.
  2. The distance covered during the time interval from 0.75 hr to 1 hr is 8.75 km.
  3. During the time interval from 0 to 0.5 hrs, the train moves with constant velocity.
questions.explanation:
1. As the part BC of the speed-time graph has maximum slope, so acceleration is maximum during this interval.
    Maximum acceleration = Slope of BC
    = {tex}\frac{(50-20) \mathrm{km} \mathrm{h}^{-1}}{(1.00-0.75) \mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {30}{0.25}{/tex} = 120 km h-2
  2. Distance covered from 0.75 h to 1.00 h
    = Area of trapezium BEFC
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}[BE + CF] {tex}\times{/tex} EF
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}[20 + 50] {tex}\times{/tex} (1.00 - 0.75) km = 8.75 km

questions.question: A man walks on a straight road from his home to a market 5.5 km away with a speed of 5.5 km h-1. Finding the market closed he instantly turns and walks back with a speed of 7.5 km h-1. What is the average speed and average velocity of the man between t = 0 to t = 104 min?
questions.explanation:
Time taken by the man to go from his home to the market, t1 = {tex}\frac{5.5 \mathrm{~km}}{5.5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}}{/tex} = 1.0 h
Time taken by the man to return back from the market to his home, t2 = {tex}\frac{5.5 \mathrm{~km}}{7.5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.73 h
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total time taken = t1 + t2 = 1.0 h + 0.73 h = 1.73 h = 140 min
In t = 0 to 104 min, total distance travelled = 5.5 km + 5.5 km = 11 km
Displacement = 0 (As the boy returns back home)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Average speed
= {tex}\frac{\text { Distance travelled }}{\text { Time taken }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{11 \mathrm{~km}}{1.73 \mathrm{~h}}{/tex} = 6.36 km h-1
Average velocity = {tex}\frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time taken }}{/tex} = 0

questions.question: Two racing cars of masses m1 and m2 are moving in circles of radii r1 and r2 respectively. Their speeds are such that each makes a complete circle in the same length of time T. The ratio of angular speed of the first car to that of the second car is
questions.explanation:
Both the cars make a complete circle in the same time T, which means that angular speeds of both the cars are same.
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega{/tex} = {tex}\frac vr{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r}{T r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 r}{T }{/tex}

questions.question: The speed of a train increases at a constant rate a from zero to v, and then remains constant for an interval, and finally decreases to zero at a constant rate p. If L be the total distance travelled, then the total time taken is
questions.explanation:
Velocity increases from 0 to v:
We know that v = u + at
Here, u = 0, v = v, a = {tex}\alpha{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} t = {tex}\frac v {\alpha}{/tex}
and s = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} at2; s1 = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 \alpha}{/tex}
Velocity decreases from v to 0:
Here, u = v, v = 0, a = -{tex}\beta{/tex}
Using v = u + at
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{v}{\beta}{/tex}
and s = ut + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}at2; s2 = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 \beta}{/tex}
So, distance travelled during acceleration and retardation,
d = s1 + s2 = {tex}​​\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}
Thus, distance travelled during constant velocity
S3 = L -{tex}​​\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}
So, time taken to travel this distance
{tex}t=\frac{L-\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)}{v}{/tex} = {tex}\frac Lv{/tex} - {tex}\frac v2{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}
Hence, total time taken to cover distance L
= {tex}\left(\frac{v}{\alpha}\right){/tex} + {tex}\left[\frac{L}{v}-\frac{v}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right)\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left(\frac{v}{\beta}\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac Lv{/tex} + {tex}\frac 2v{/tex}{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\alpha}+\frac{1}{\beta}\right){/tex}

questions.question: A train starts from a station P and travels some distance with a uniform acceleration a1, then it goes with uniform retardation a2 for some more distance and come to rest at the station Q. If the distance between the stations P and Q is 4 km and the train takes 4 minutes to complete this journey, then {tex}\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}{/tex} =
questions.explanation:
For motion with uniform acceleration a1:
From v = u + at
v = a1t1 ({tex}\because{/tex} u = 0), {tex}\therefore{/tex} t1 = {tex}\frac{v}{a_1}{/tex} ...(i)
and from s = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}at2,
Using eqn. (i)
s1 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}a1{tex}\left(\frac{v}{a_1}\right)^2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 a_1}{/tex} ...(ii)
For motion with uniform retardation a2:
From v = u + at
v = a2t2 ({tex}\because{/tex} v = 0, u = v, a = -a2)
t2 = {tex}\left(\frac{v}{a_2}\right){/tex} ...(iii)
and from s = ut + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}at2
s2 = {tex}v \frac{v}{a_2}+\frac{1}{2}\left(-a_2\right) \cdot \frac{v^2}{a_2^2}{/tex}
s2 = {tex}\frac{v^2}{a_2}-\frac{v^2}{2 a_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{v^2}{2 \mathrm{a}_2}{/tex} ...(iv)
Given, s1 + s2 = 4 km and t1 + t2 = 4 min, so using (ii) and (iv),
{tex}\frac{v^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}\right){/tex}= 4 ...(v)
and v{tex}\left(\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}\right){/tex} = 4 ...(vi)
Dividing eqn. (v) by eqn. (vi), we get v = 2
Putting this in eqn. (vi),
{tex}\frac{1}{a_1}+\frac{1}{a_2}{/tex} = 2{tex}\frac{\mathrm{min}^2}{\mathrm{~km}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 3600}{1000} \frac{s^2}{m}{/tex}
= 7.2 m-1 s2

questions.question: The diagram shows the velocity-time graph of two moving cars P and Q. The graph indicates that

options.option: The velocity of car P is increasing at a decreasing rate from 40 s to 45 s in same direction.
Acceleration of the car Q is not zero at any point during whole journey.
Car Q is moving with a constant acceleration from 0 to 40 seconds.

questions.explanation: From 40 s to 45 s, direction of car P is same but velocity is increasing at a decreasing rate of acceleration.

questions.question: After jumping out from the plane, a parachutist falls 80 m without friction. When he opens up the parachute, he decelerates at 2 m s-2. He reaches the ground with a speed of 4 m s-1. How long did the parachutist spend his time in the air? (Take g = 10 m s-2)
questions.explanation:
Time taken when he falls 80 m in the air is t1, and U1= 0, a1=10 m s-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} d = U1t1 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}a_1 t_1^2{/tex} or 80 = 0 + {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10{tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}t_1^2{/tex}
u1 = 0
a1 = 10 m s-2
t1 = ?
u2 = v1
a2 = 2 m s-2
t2 = ?
u2 = 4 m s-1
or {tex}t_1^2{/tex} = 16; t1 = 4 s {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v1 = u1 + a1t1
v1 = 0 + 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 40 m s-1
Since v1 = u2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} u2 = 40 m s-1
Let the time taken after he opens up his parachute and before reaches the ground is t2.
v2 = 4 ms-1, a2 = -2 m s-2

v2 = u2 + a2t2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 = 40 + (-2) {tex}\times{/tex} t2
2t2 = 36; t2 = 18 s
Total time = 4 s + 18 s = 22 s

questions.question: What is the S.I. unit of Momentum?
questions.explanation:
The units of momentum are the product of the units of mass and velocity.
Linear Momentum = Mass {tex}\times{/tex} Velocity
S.I Unit of Momentum = S.I Unit of mass {tex}\times{/tex} S.I Unit of velocity
S.I Unit of Momentum = kg {tex}\times{/tex} m.s-1
S.I Unit of Momentum = kg.m.s-1

questions.question: What is the numerical formula for Force?
questions.explanation:
Force is an interaction between objects that causes them to change motion.
The formula for force states that force is equal to mass multiplied by acceleration. So, if you know mass and acceleration, just multiply them together and now you know the force!
Then, F = ma
The units for acceleration are meters per second square( m/s2), and the units for mass are kilograms (kg).

questions.question: If the initial velocity is zero then the force acting is ________.
questions.explanation: Acceleration

questions.question: Which of the following statement is not correct for an object moving along a straight path in an accelerated motion?

options.option: A force is always acting on it
It always goes away from the earth

questions.explanation: It always goes away from the earth

questions.question: Newton's First Law of Motion is also called as:-
questions.explanation: Newton's first law of motion is also referred to as the Law of Inertia. An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

questions.question: If the friction acting on the body is more, the body will move:
questions.explanation: Less speedily as it cancels the force which moves it as friction is the force acting opposite to the moving body and frictional force will never be more because frictional force exerts an equal force back to the body.

questions.question: The S.I. unit of Weight is:
questions.explanation: Weight is the result of a mass experiencing the force of gravity, so the SI 'unit of weight' is also Newtons.

questions.question: Which equation defines Newton's Second Law of Motion?
questions.explanation:
The second law is the one that tells you how to calculate the value of a force. Newton's second law allows you to work out how a force when it is present, will affect the motion of an object.
F = ma = {tex}\frac{{dp}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
Force is directly proportional to the rate of change of momentum.

questions.question: The people on the bus are pushed back when the bus starts suddenly due to:
questions.explanation:
Each body has an inertia of its own. It is an ability to resist any change in its state of motion.
So when a person is sitting on the bus, he is at rest. But the lower part of the body is attached to the bus which is in motion. Thus, the lower part of the body attached to the bus seat starts moving but the upper part resists the motion and that is why we fall backward.

questions.question: If the force acting on the body is zero, its momentum is ________.
questions.explanation:
Force,  F =  {tex}\frac{dp}{dt}{/tex} ( Rate of change of momentum).
Therefore, only when the momentum is constant, will the acting force be Zero.

questions.question: The inability of the body to change its state of rest or motion is called:
questions.explanation: An object does not change its state of rest or uniform motion on its own. The inability of any object to change its state is called Inertia.

questions.question: Which law explains swimming?
questions.explanation:
Newton’s third law says that for every reaction, there is an equal and opposite reaction.
One body cannot exert a force on another without experiencing a force itself

questions.question: A batsman hits a cricket ball which then rolls on a level ground. After covering a short distance, the ball comes to rest. The ball stops because

options.option: The batsman did not hit the ball hard enough.
There is no unbalanced force on the ball, so the ball would want to come to rest.
Velocity is proportional to the force exerted on the ball.
There is a force on the ball opposing the motion.

questions.explanation: The ball slows down and comes to rest due to opposing forces of air resistance and frictional force on the ball opposing its motion.

questions.question: What is the momentum of an object of mass m, moving with a velocity v?
questions.explanation:
Momentum is mass in motion and only applies to objects in motion.

Thus,

Momentum = Mass * Velocity

questions.question: A bullet of mass 10 g is fired from a rifle. The bullet tales 0.003 second to move through its barrel and leaves it with a velocity of 300 m/s. What is the force exerted on the bullet by the rifle?
questions.explanation:
Mass of bullet = 10 g = {tex}\frac{10}{1000}{/tex} kg.
initial velocity (u) = 0 and final velocity (v) = 300 m/s.
time (t) = 0.003 s
F= {tex}\frac{(mv - mu)}{t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(0.01 \times 300 - 0.01 \times 0) }{0.003}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(3- 0)}{0.003}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{0.003}{/tex} 
= 1000 N

questions.question: A force of 5 N gives a mass m1 an acceleration of 10ms-2 and mass m2 an acceleration of 20 ms-2. What acceleration would if give it both the masses were tied together?
questions.explanation:
m1 = {tex}\frac{5N}{10 ms^{-2}}{/tex} = 0.5 kg, m2 = {tex}\frac{5N}{20 ms^{-2}}{/tex} = 0.25 kg 
Total mass = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 kg.
Acceleration produced when both masses are tied together = {tex}\frac{5 N}{0.75 kg}{/tex} kg
= 6.67 ms-2.

questions.question:
Inflated balloon lying on the surface of a floor moves forward when pierced with a pin.
The above-mentioned phenomena is due to
questions.explanation: Inflated balloon lying on the surface of a floor moves forward when pierced with a pin due to Newton’s third law of motion. The air coming out of the balloon forces the balloon in the opposite direction.

questions.question: The change in the momentum of a body in 0.01 seconds is 10 kg ms-1. The force acting on this body is
questions.explanation: The force acting on a body is equal to rate of change of momentum. Change of momentum = 10 Kg/s, time taken = 0.01 second. Force = {tex}\frac{10}{0.01}{/tex} = 1000 N.

questions.question: If an object experiences a net zero unbalanced force then the body:
questions.explanation: If an object experiences a net-zero unbalanced force, then the body moves with constant velocity. Zero unbalanced forces produce no acceleration in the body and the body continues to move with the same velocity.

questions.question: A man is at rest in the middle of the pond on perfectly smooth ice. He can get himself to the shore by making use of Newton's:
questions.explanation: By using Newton’s third law of motion, he can get himself to shore. When the person pushes forward, the reaction force offered by the ice is very little. But, this reaction force will help him to move forward. Hence, Newton's Third Law will help him to reach the shore.

questions.question: A force can be completely described by:
questions.explanation: A force can cause an object with mass to change its velocity (which includes beginning moving from a state of rest), i.e., to accelerate. Force can also be described intuitively as a push or a pull. A force has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity.

questions.question: The SI unit of force is:
questions.explanation: The newton (symbol: N) is the International System of Units (SI) derived unit of force. It is named after Isaac Newton in recognition of his work on classical mechanics, specifically Newton's second law of motion.

questions.question: Which of the following has the largest inertia?
questions.explanation: Inertia is the resistance of any physical object to any change in its state of motion. This includes changes to the object's speed, direction, or state of rest. Inertia is proportional to mass. Big masses resist changing their motion more than smaller masses. Schoolbag has the largest inertia because the heavier body has larger inertia.

questions.question: The unit of momentum can be expressed as
questions.explanation:
The Newton second (also newton-second, symbol Ns or N·s)is the derived SI unit of Momentum. It is dimensionally equivalent to the momentum unit kilogram metre per second (kgm/s). One newton second corresponds to a one-newton force applied for one second.
{tex}\vec F.t=\Delta m\vec v{/tex}

questions.question: A jet engine works on the principle of Newton's:
questions.explanation: The jet engine sucks air in at the front with a fan. A compressor raises the pressure of the air. The compressor is made with many blades attached to a shaft. The blades spin at high speed and compress or squeeze the air. The compressed air is then sprayed with fuel and an electric spark lights the mixture. The burning gases expand and blast out through the nozzle, at the back of the engine. As the jets of gas shoot backward, the engine and the aircraft are thrust forward (Newton's third law of motion).

questions.question: Action reaction forces act:
questions.explanation: Reaction from a body is caused due to action of some another body on it.

questions.question: The acceleration of a body is to be doubled from its initial value. By what factor is the acting force to be increased?
questions.explanation: Force is directly proportional to the acceleration.

questions.question: According to second law of Newton, force is the cause and the outcome is:
questions.explanation: The acceleration of an object as produced by a net force is directly proportional to the magnitude of the net force, in the same direction as the net force, and inversely proportional to the mass of the object.

questions.question: If force, change in momentum and time are given by F, p and t respectively, then they are related by
questions.explanation: {tex}F=\frac{P}{t}{/tex}because momentum can be given as the product of the force applied and time.

questions.question: The one which has the least inertia among the following: 1 kg stone, 2 kg ball, a train compartment and a cup of tea is
questions.explanation: Inertia is directly proportional to mass and one which poses least resistance also possess least inertia.

questions.question: The minimum number of unequal forces that can make zero resultant is
questions.explanation: If two unequal forces are taken then given two vectors of unequal length, adding them will result in a non-zero vector because, in order to have a zero vector sum, the first and second vectors must "cancel out." For example, if you travel left 1 metre you must travel 1 metre right to return to your initial position. So, 3 unequal forces are mandatory for making resultant force zero.

questions.question: On a 3 kg mass, 5 newton of force acts for 0.1 second. The impulse imparted to the mass is (in kg m/s):
questions.explanation:
Impulse can also be expressed as the rate of change of momentum.
And Momentum= force × time =5 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1
=0.5 kg m/s

questions.question:
The two states of motion treated alike by Newton's first law, among A, B, C, and D are
A : Rest B: Uniform motion C: Uniformly accelerated. D : Non-uniformly accelerated
questions.explanation: Newton's first law deals with rest or uniform motion of a body,i.e; An object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

questions.question: Water drops sticking to the wheel come out along the tangential line due to
questions.explanation: Inertia is the resistance of any physical object to any change in its state of motion. This includes changes to the object's speed, direction, or state of rest.

questions.question: If 10 kg and 5 N are the S.I. units of mass and force respectively, the S.I. unit of acceleration is
questions.explanation:
F= m {tex}\times{/tex} a
a= F/m
So, a= 0.5 m/s2

questions.question: A 20 kg gun fires a bullet of mass 20 g with a velocity of 400 m/s. The action on the shoulder of the person per second by the gun is:
questions.explanation:
In the given problem, 
Mass of gun  =20kg ;  velocity of bullet =400m/s 
mass of the bullet = 20g = 0.02 kg
F= change in momentum/ time taken 
For t = 1sec. 
F=  m× v /t
=400× 0.02/1
=8N

questions.question: The acceleration of a body has to be doubled without any change in force. Then one has to
questions.explanation:
Acceleration = force / mass
If force is to be kept constant then, mass must be reduced to half to make acceleration double.

questions.question: A truck carries sand. Due to hole in the base of truck, significant amount of sand falls continuously. For the same condition of working of the engine, the speed of the truck should
questions.explanation: Speed of truck should be increased to cause minimum loss

questions.question: A force of 5 N applied on m1 produces an acceleration of 8 m/s2 and when applied on m2 produces an acceleration of 24 m/s2. When they are tied together, the acceleration will be
questions.explanation:
For the first body,
F = m1a​​​​​​​​1
So, 5 = m1 {tex}\times{/tex} 8
So, m1 = {tex}5\over 8{/tex} kg
For second body,
F = m​​​​​​2​​​​​a​2
So, 5 = m2 {tex}\times{/tex} 24
So, m2 = {tex}5\over 24{/tex} kg
Combined mass of both bodies, m1 + m2
= {tex}5\over8{/tex} + {tex}5\over 24{/tex} = {tex}20\over 24{/tex} kg
Now, m = {tex}20\over 24{/tex} kg
F = 5N
a = ?
F = ma
So, 5 = {tex}20 \over 24{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} a
So, a = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}24 \over 20{/tex}
So, a = 6m/s2

questions.question: Fireman holds a hose by exerting a _____.
questions.explanation: Fireman holds a hose by exerting a force because equal and opposite force acts on him. To counter that force he needs to hold the hose with force.

questions.question: Bags at the top of school van are tied using a string to avoid the effect of
questions.explanation: Inertia is the resistance of any physical object to any change in its state of motion. This includes changes to the object's speed, direction, or state of rest. Inertia will result in falling off the bag from the top of the van.

questions.question: While catching a stone thrown by your friend you pull the hands back to
questions.explanation: To increase the time to slow down so that jerk is minimised and injury is avoided.

questions.question: A plate, a ball and child all have the same mass. The one having more inertia is the
questions.explanation: Inertia is directly proportional to mass, hence all the above given objects will have equal inertia.

questions.question: When no external force acts on an object, the physical quantity that remains conserved is
questions.explanation: Momentum remains conserved in absence of any external force. Momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object. It is a three-dimensional vector quantity, possessing a magnitude and a direction.

questions.question: The inertia of an object tends to cause the object

options.option: to resists any change in its state of motion

questions.explanation: Inertia resists any change in its state of motion.It is a property of matter by which it continues in its existing state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line, unless that the state is changed by any external force.

questions.question: A passenger in a moving train tosses a coin which falls behind him. It means that motion of the train is
questions.explanation: Coin falls behind due to inertia of motion.Inertia  is an inherent property of an object to resist any change in its state of rest or of uniform motion.

questions.question: An object of mass 2 kg is sliding with a constant velocity of 4ms-1 on a frictionless horizontal table. The force required to keep the object moving with the same velocity is
questions.explanation: According to the first law of motion, an object at rest stays at rest and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force

questions.question: A water tanker filled up to {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}rd of its height is moving with a uniform speed. On sudden application of the brake, the water in the tank would
questions.explanation: Water moves forward due to inertia of motion. Inertia is an inherent property of an object to resist any change in its state of rest or of uniformmotion.

questions.question: A mass M breaks into two pieces in the ratio 1 : 3 while at rest. If the heavier has a speed of v, the speed of the lighter is
questions.explanation:
Due to the conservation of momentum a collision between two bodies, the total momentum of the colliding bodies before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision.
m1v1= m2v2
m1=1, m2 = 3, v2 = v
V1 = 3v

questions.question: When a 12 N force acts on 3 kg mass for a second, the change in velocity is (in m/s)
questions.explanation: {tex}v\;=\frac{F\times t}{mass}=\frac{12\times1}3=\;4 m/s{/tex}

questions.question: A cup of water holding a steel ball (m) is allowed to fall under gravity. The reaction between the ball and the base of the cup is
questions.explanation: No reaction occurs between the ball and the base of the cup.

questions.question: Impulse has the S.I. unit of ______
questions.explanation: If we multiply the force acting on an object by the time it is acting for this is called the impulse of a force. Impulse is a vector quantity and its unit is the kilogram meter per second (kgms-1) or the newton second (Ns).

questions.question: According to the third law of motion, action and reaction

options.option: act on either body at normal to each other
always act on different bodies in opposite directions

questions.explanation: According to Newton's 3rd law of motion , action and reaction are of equal magnitude and directed in opposite direction.

questions.question: The increase in velocity acquired by a 10 kg mass in 5 seconds due to a force of 10 newton is
questions.explanation:
{tex}{l}F=\frac{m\times v}t\\{/tex}
{tex}10\;=\frac{10\;\times v}5{/tex}
{tex}v=\frac{50}{10}\;=\;5m/s{/tex}

questions.question: A goalkeeper in a game of football pulls his hands backwards after holding the ball shot at the goal. This enables the goalkeeper to

options.option: reduce the force exerted by the ball on hands
increase the rate of change of momentum
decrease the rate of change of momentum

questions.explanation: The goalkeeper pulls his hands backwards after holding the ball to decrease the rate of change of momentum by increasing the time. By doing this, less force is exerted on his hands (v Force is directly proportional to the rate of change of momentum).

questions.question: A motorcycle and a car are moving on a horizontal road with the same velocity. If they are brought to rest by the application of brakes, which provided equal retardation, then

options.option: Car will stop at a shorter distance
Both will stop at the same distance

questions.explanation: As both are moving with same velocity and retardation provided by brakes is equal, so distance travelled would be same for both motorcycle and car.

questions.question: In a rocket, fuel burns at the rate of 1 kg s-1. This fuel is ejected from the rocket with a velocity of 60 km s-1. This exerts a force on the rocket equal to
questions.explanation:
Here, rate of burning fuel,{tex}\frac{d m}{d t}{/tex}= 1 kg s-1
Velocity of fuel ejection, v = 60 km s-1 = 60000 m s-1
Thrust ={tex}\frac{d m}{d t}{/tex}.v = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 60000 = 60000 N
Thus, force exerted on rocket is 60000 N.

questions.question: A player caught a cricket ball of mass 200 g moving at a rate 60 m/s. If the catching process completes in 0.4 s, the force of the blow exerted by the ball on the hand of the player is equal to
questions.explanation:
Given m = 200 g = 0.2 kg, u = 60 m/s, t = 0.4 s
Here, a ={tex}\frac{v-u}{t}{/tex}={tex}\frac{0-60}{0.4}{/tex}= 150 m s-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex}F = ma = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 150 = 30 N

questions.question: Consider two spring balances hooked as shown in the figure. We pull them in opposite directions. If the reading shown by A is 1.5 N, the reading shown by B will be
questions.explanation: According to Newton’s third law, the reading shown by B should be same as A, i.e., 1.5 N.

questions.question:
Which of the following options is correct?

  1. Force of gravity on a body is equal to the true weight of the body.
  2. A person standing in an elevator moving downwards with constant acceleration finds his weight less than actual.
questions.explanation: For a lift moving downward with acceleration a, Acceleration of body moving = g - a

questions.question: A ball is rolling down a slope at a steady speed. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.option: There are no forces acting on the ball.
Frictional force is greater than the forward force.
The forces acting on the ball are balanced.

questions.explanation: When all the forces are balanced or the net force is zero, objects move with steady speed.

questions.question: Which of the following is the velocity-time graph of a moving particle on which net external force is zero?
questions.explanation: Net external force is zero, it means, particle is moving with constant velocity. Velocity-time graph of such a particle is represented by a straight line parallel to time axis.

questions.question: In the first second of its flight, a rocket ejects {tex}\frac 1{40}{/tex} of its mass with a velocity of 1200 m s-1. The acceleration of the rocket is
questions.explanation:
Let m be the mass of the object.
Ejected mass of the rocket = {tex}\frac m {40}{/tex}
According to the law of conservation of momentum,
mu1 ={tex}\frac m {40}{/tex}u2; at = {tex}\frac{u_2}{40} {/tex}; a = {tex}\frac{1200}{40 \times 1}{/tex} = 30 m s-2
({tex}\because{/tex} u2 = 1200 m s-1 and t = 1 second)

questions.question:
Statement 1: If a person push a massive truck parked along the roadside by using its muscular force only, it will not move.
Statement 2: Two opposite and equal forces acted on two bodies in contact cancel each other.

options.option: Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false but statement 2 is true.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation: Force of action reaction never acts on one body. Truck does not move because the push applied is far less than the force of friction between the truck and the road.

questions.question: Three identical bodies each of mass m, are pushed by a force F on a frictionless table as shown in figure.

options.option: Force applied by Y on Z is equal to net force on X.
Net force on Y is equal to zero.
Force applied by X on Y is {tex}\frac F3{/tex}.

questions.explanation:
Let a be the common acceleration, then F = 3ma or a = {tex}\frac F {3m}{/tex}
Net force on X = ma = m {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac F {3m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac F {3}{/tex}
Force applied by X on Y = (m + m)a = 2m {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac F {3m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {2F} {3}{/tex}
Force applied by Y on Z = ma = m {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac F {3m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac F {3}{/tex}

questions.question: Two blocks made of different metals identical in shape and size are acted upon by equal forces which cause them to slide on a horizontal surface.
