The acceleration of the second block is found to 10 times that of the first. What is the ratio of the mass of the second to the first?
questions.explanation:
{tex}\frac{\text { Acceleration of first block, } a_1}{\text { Acceleration of second block, } a_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Force is same on both the blocks.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} F = m1a1= m2a2
{tex}\frac{a_1}{a_2} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{m_2}{m_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_2}{m_1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}

questions.question: Even though stone also attracts earth towards itself, the earth does not move:
questions.explanation: Acceleration is inversely proportional to mass according to Newton's 3rd law of motion. So, a small stone produces large acceleration. Due to the large acceleration of stone, it falls towards Earth.

questions.question: The weight of an object is

options.option: Greater on earth and lesser on Moon
Not equal on both earth and Moon
Lesser on earth and Greater on earth

questions.explanation:
Weight depends on gravity, W = mg
As the gravity of Earth is greater than the gravity of the moon, the object weighs more on Earth.

questions.question: The weight of an object has S.I. unit of:
questions.explanation:
The force of attraction of Earth on a body is the same as the weight of the body.
Therefore, the Weight of an object is measured in the same unit as Force.

questions.question: If cross - sectional area of an object is more than the pressure applied by the external force is :-
questions.explanation: Pressure is inversely proportional to cross- sectional area.

questions.question: Weight of the object is:

options.option: More at poles and less at equator
More at the equator and less at poles

questions.explanation:
As the radius of Earth is maximum at the equator, the value of g is less at the equator by g =  G.Mm/R2
W = mg, If g is less weight will be less. Therefore, weight is less at the equator.

questions.question: The S.I. units of mass, force, and weight are respectively:
questions.explanation:
Force of attraction of the earth on an object is the same as that of the weight of the object. So, they have the same units.
Mass of an object is measured in Kg.

questions.question: Unit of g is ________.
questions.explanation: Acceleration is a change in velocity per time. Change in velocity is m/s and time is measured in seconds, thus reaching the conclusion m/s2.

questions.question: The relation between the weight of an object on the moon (wm) and on the earth (We) is:
questions.explanation: Weight depends on gravity and gravity on the moon is {tex}1 \over 6{/tex}th the gravity on earth. Thus this relation.

questions.question: S. I. unit of Pressure is ________.
questions.explanation: The pressure is defined as the Thrust/Area. Thrust (Force ) is measured in Newton and Area in m2.

questions.question: S.I. Unit of pressure is ________.
questions.explanation: SI unit of measuring pressure is newtons per square metre. N/m2 is known as Pascal.

questions.question: The pressure at a point in the liquid is:
questions.explanation: The pressure is transmitted undiminished in any direction.

questions.question: The upward force experienced by a body when submerged in a fluid is called
questions.explanation: The upward force experienced by the body when submerged in a fluid is called buoyant force. Pressure increases with depth as a result of the weight of the overlying fluid. So, the pressure at the bottom of an object submerged in a fluid is greater at the top of the object. This pressure difference results in a net upwards force on the object. Buoyant force depends upon nature of fluid and density of substance.

questions.question: If a planet existed whose mass and radius were both half that of the earth, the acceleration due to gravity at its surface would be:
questions.explanation:
A very simple relation relates to the acceleration due to gravity with the mass (M) and radius (R) of a planet. It is given as
{tex}g ={GM\over R^2} {/tex}
Now, for earth g = 9.8 ms-2 but for a planet with mass and radius half of Earth
{tex}g' ={ G{M\over 2}\over ({R\over 2})^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow g' = 2{GM\over R^2} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} g' = 2g
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} g' = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 = 19.6 ms2.

questions.question: A stone is dropped from a cliff. Its speed after it has fallen 100 m is:
questions.explanation:
Intial Velocity u = 0 m/s
Final Velocity v =?
Distance covered S = 100 m
Acceleration a = 10m/s2
As we know,
v​​​​​​2 - u​​​​​​2 =2aS
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v2 − 0 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v2 = 2000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 44.2 m/s

questions.question: The weight of an object:

options.option: is the force as its mass but expressed in different units
is the quantity of matter it contains
is the force with which it is attracted to the earth.

questions.explanation:
Weight is the force with which its is attracted to the earth.
And, F = m {tex}\times{/tex} a
i.e. W = m {tex}\times{/tex} g

questions.question: If the distance between two objects is doubled, the gravitational force between them:
questions.explanation:
We know
{tex}G ={ Mm\over r^2}{/tex} 
Where r is the distance between two objects. 
So If the distance between two objects is doubled, the gravitational force between them becomes one fourth because G is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the two bodies.

questions.question: A bomb is released from an aircraft. Its trajectory is
questions.explanation: As the aircraft is at some height and moving forward so, the trajectory will be parabola because the gravity acts in perpendicular direction.

questions.question: A soft drink bottle is dropped by a waiter from the top of a hotel whose height is 100 m. It takes T second to reach the ground. Where will it be at {tex}\frac T 2{/tex} second?
questions.explanation:
In T sec. bottle travels H m which is equal to height of hotel.
by applying equations of motion we get
S = {tex}ut + \frac 1 2gT^2 {/tex}
h = {tex}\frac 1 2gT^2{/tex} ...(i)
Let in {tex}{T\over 2}{/tex} s it cover  h1 
So, h1 = {tex}\frac 1 2g \frac {T^2}{ 4} {/tex} ....(ii)
From [i] and [ii] we get
h1 = {tex}\frac{h}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h1 = {tex}\frac{100}{4}{/tex} = 25 m
Therefore distance from ground is 100 - 25 = 75 m

questions.question: Force of gravitation between two bodies varies with 'r' as
questions.explanation:
Force of gravitation
F = Gm​​​​​1​​​​m​​​2/ r​​​​​​2

questions.question: At which of the locations the value of 'g' is a maximum?
questions.explanation: The value of 'g' is Maximum where the radius is minimum i.e. near poles.

questions.question: Consider earth to be a homogeneous sphere. Scientist A goes deep down in a mine and scientist B goes high up in a balloon. The acceleration due to gravity as measured by

options.option: B goes on decreasing, A goes on increasing
A goes on decreasing, B goes on increasing

questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the person and the center of the earth.

questions.question: Which of the following is the evidence to show that there must be a force acting on the earth and directed towards the sun

options.option: apparent motion of sun around the earth
revolution of the earth around the sun
deviation of falling bodies towards the east

questions.explanation: The revolution of the earth around the sun takes place in a trajectory orbit. The average distance between the earth and the sun is approximately 150 million km.

questions.question: If the earth stops rotating the value of 'g' at the equator will
questions.explanation: The value of 'g' will slightly increase because if the earth stops rotating then it will be more spherical.

questions.question: The value of gravitational constant depends upon
questions.explanation: The gravitational constant is independent of mass, distance, and temperature.

questions.question: In vacuum all freely falling objects
questions.explanation: Air is absent in vacuum so no change will take place in acceleration to a freely falling body irrespective of their nature or mass.

questions.question: When a spaceship is two earth radii distant from the centre of the earth, its gravitational acceleration is
questions.explanation: g is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the earth and the body. As the distance gets twice the g will become one - fourth.

questions.question: The equation F = {tex}\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}{/tex} is valid only for
questions.explanation: spherical bodies, because it takes into consideration the radius of the body also.

questions.question: Two bodies, one held 1 m vertically above the other, are released simultaneously and fall freely under gravity. After 2 second, the relative separation of the bodies will be
questions.explanation: Relative positions remain constant irrespective of the distance travelled and time taken during free fall.

questions.question: An apple and a stone dropped from certain height accelerates

options.option: depends on the position of the SUN

questions.explanation:
Acceleration= {tex}{change\ in\ velocity}\over{time\ taken}{/tex}

Thus the change in velocity with respect to the time taken will be equal in each of the respective cases.

questions.question: A 60 kg man weight W kg on the moon. W is
questions.explanation: Gravity on the Moon has approximately 1/6th of the strength of gravity on Earth, a man who weighs 60kg on Earth would weigh approximately 10kg on the Moon. His mass, however, remains constant.

questions.question: A mass weighing W can float if the upthrust U is related as
questions.explanation: Weight is a force acting on an object and always acts downwards. Upthrust is a force exerted by fluid in an upward direction. For an object to float both of these forces act simultaneously and eventually get balanced with each other.

questions.question: In a tank having 'h' height of liquid, two points at a depth{tex}\frac{3h}{4}{/tex} from free surface have pressure PA and PB. When point A is closer to the wall than B. Then
questions.explanation: Pressure varies with height of liquid in a container and height is same for both the points so pressure at both points remain same.

questions.question: The least value of apparent weight of a body in a fluid is

options.option: depends on the density of solid and fluid

questions.explanation: When a body floats in a liquid, upthrust acting on the body is equal to the weight of the body in accordance with the law of flotation. That is, the weight of the body is balanced by the upthrust. So, the apparent weight of the body is zero as the net force acting on the body is zero.

questions.question: Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the moon is {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}th of its value on the surface of the earth as they have different
questions.explanation: Density, mass and radius of moon is different than earth so the acceleration due to gravity is different and calculatively obtained 1/6th of Earth's gravity.

questions.question: Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth is the greatest
questions.explanation: F = G (mM/R²), acts along the line joining the centre of the earth and the centre of the body. This force produces an acceleration 'g' in the body of mass m. Also, Acceleration due to gravity is proportional to the mean density of the earth, and its radius.

questions.question: Acceleration due to gravity varies with
questions.explanation:
Acceleration due to gravity varies with height, depth and shape of the planet as follows-

  • As the height (altitude) increases acceleration due to gravity (g) decreases.
  • As the depth increases acceleration due to gravity (g) decreases.
  • Acceleration due to gravity is inversely proportional to the radius.

questions.question: A heavier and a lighter body have equal momentum, then
questions.explanation: The lighter body will have more K.E because kinetic energy is inversely proportional to the mass of the body.

questions.question: A heavy cylinder of length 1 is slowly taken out of a dense liquid. The weight felt as it is taken out of the liquid

options.option: increases till it attains the weight in air

questions.explanation:
Increases till it attains the weight in air as-
The buoyant force exerted by a fluid exerts upwards on a body submerged in it,
F= {tex}\rho{/tex}V
F = {tex}\rho{/tex}Vg
where ρ is the density of the fluid, V is the volume of the fluid displaced, and 'g' is the acceleration due to gravity.
The liquid pushes the solid upward and by Newton's third law, there is a force of equal magnitude acting downward on the liquid.

questions.question: Any object having sufficient positive energy can escape from the gravitational pull of the earth as

options.option: P.E. is positive for total energy being positive
K.E. will be more to escape the gravitational potential energy

questions.explanation: Negative energy means it is bound and the work done by the gravity is the positive inbound state when it is approached by some mass. The mass gets trapped under the potential energy of gravity and is not allowed to escape.

questions.question: A stone is dropped from the top of the tower. Its speed after it has fallen 20 m is (Take g = 10ms-1 )
questions.explanation:
V​​​​​​2 - u​​​​​​2 = 2gs
u = 0 g  g = 10ms-1, s = 20 m
V2 = 2 × 10 × 20 = 400
V = 20 m/s

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward, acceleration due to gravity is

options.option: In the direction opposite to the direction of motion.
In the direction same as the direction motion.

questions.explanation: When a ball is thrown then the direction of motion of the ball and vertically upwards then the direction of motion velocity is the same, i.e. upward of the ball is upwards but the acceleration due to gravity acts downwards towards the earth. Thus, the direction of acceleration is opposite to the direction of motion of the ball.

questions.question: A ball is thrown up and attains a maximum height of 100 m. It is thrown upwards with a speed of
questions.explanation:
V​​​​​​2 - u​​​​​​2 = 2gs
u = 0 g , g = 10ms-1, s = 100 m
V2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 2000
V = 44.2 m/s

questions.question: A stone dropped from a certain height takes 4 second to reach the ground. The height is
questions.explanation:
S = ut + {tex}\frac12{/tex} gt2 where S = distance, U = initial velocity, t = time and g = acceleration due to gravity.
Given: U = 0 m/sec, t = 4sec.
we know g= 9.8 m/sec2
Therefore, S = {tex}\frac12{/tex}gt2
 S = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8{tex}\times{/tex} 16
S = 78.4 meter.

questions.question: A body at rest having uniform acceleration is

options.option: any body thrown vertically up against gravity.
can be released only on the moon.

questions.explanation: When an object is thrown vertically upwards at the maximum height the velocity is going to be zero, however, acceleration is not zero. If there is no velocity, there can not be acceleration.

questions.question: Increase in momentum by 20% will increase the K.E. by
questions.explanation:
Momentum mv increase by 20% if velocity v increase by 1.2 v.
Now, kinetic energy k = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2, increase KE = {tex}\frac12{/tex} m(1.2v)2 – {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2 = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2 (1.44 - 1) = 0.44 {tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2 = 0.44 K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Percentage increases in KE = {tex}\frac{0.44k}k{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 44%

questions.question: Earth goes round the Sun with
questions.explanation: The Earth's orbit is not perfectly circular. As a result its velocity changes slightly over the course of the year.

questions.question: If the weight of an object in air and a dense liquid are Wa and Wl respectively, then upthrust
questions.explanation:
Up thrust = apparent lost in weight of the object
Up thrust = Wa - Wl

questions.question: Acceleration due to gravity (due to the shell) at any point inside the shell is

options.option: zero as long as no other mass is inside.
zero as long as no other mass is outside the shell.

questions.explanation: The gravitational force on an object of mass placed inside a spherical shell of matter is zero.

questions.question: The ratio of weight to mass of the body is ________.
questions.explanation:
W = m {tex}\times{/tex} g = m {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8
{tex}\frac Wm{/tex} = 9.8

questions.question: A force acting perpendicular on an object is called as ________.
questions.explanation: Force exerted by an object perpendicular to the surface is called thrust.

questions.question: Two objects of different masses falling freely near the surface of the moon would

options.option: undergo a change in their inertia
have same velocities at any instant

questions.explanation: Object of different masses falling freely near the surface of the moon would have the same velocities at any instant because they will have same acceleration due to gravity.

questions.question: The value of acceleration due to gravity

options.option: is same on equator and poles

questions.explanation: the value of g on earth is maximum at poles and decreases as we go from poles to the equator and is minimum at the equator.

questions.question: The gravitational force between two objects is F. If masses of both objects are halved without changing distance between them, then the gravitational force would become
questions.explanation:
{tex}{F = G\frac{{M1M2}}{{{r^2}}}}{/tex}
{tex}{F = G\frac{{\frac{{{M_1}}}{2}\frac{{{M_2}}}{2}}}{{{r^2}}}}{/tex}
{tex}{F = G\frac{{M1M2}}{{4{r^2}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\begin{array}{l}F\;=\;\frac14G\frac{\displaystyle M_1M_2}{\displaystyle r^2}\\\end{array}{/tex}

questions.question: A boy is whirling a stone tied with a string in a horizontal circular path the string breaks, the stone

options.option: will move along a straight line tangential to the circular path
will move along a straight line perpendicular to the circular path away from the boy.
will continue to move in the circular path
will move along a straight line towards the centre of the circular path

questions.explanation: In circular motion, the direction of velocity at a point is always along the tangent at that point. If string breaks, then the centripetal force acting on the stone becomes zero and it will move along a straight line tangential to the circular path.

questions.question: In the relation F = {tex}\frac{Gmm}{d^2}{/tex}, the quantity G

options.option: is greater at the surface of the earth
is used only when the earth is one of the two masses
depends on the value of g at the place of observation

questions.explanation:
Law of gravitation is applicable to all bodies having some mass. And is given by F = {tex}\frac{Gmm}{d^2}{/tex}
where , F = force of attraction between the two bodies
m = mass of two bodies
G = Gravitational constant
r = Distance between the two bodies.

questions.question: Law of gravitation gives the gravitational force between

options.option: any two bodies having some mass
the earth and a point mass only

questions.explanation:
Law of gravitation is applicable to all bodies having some mass. And is given by F = {tex}\frac{Gm_1m_2}{r^2}{/tex}
where , F = force of attraction between the two bodies
m1, m2 = mass of two bodies
G = Gravitational constant
r = Distance between the two bodies.

questions.question: The value of quantity G in the law of gravitation

options.option: depends on both mass and radius of the earth
depends on mass of the earth only
depends on radius of the earth only
is independent of mass and radius of the earth

questions.explanation: The value of g is independent of the mass of the object and only dependent upon location - the planet the object is on and the distance from the center of that planet.

questions.question: The atmosphere is held to the earth by
questions.explanation: Earth's atmosphere is the layer of gases around the Earth. The atmosphere is held in place by Earth's gravity.

questions.question: The force of attraction between two unit point masses separated by a unit distance is called
questions.explanation:
We know that the gravitational force F = G {tex}\frac{Mm}{r^2}{/tex}
Where F= force between mass
m = mass of the body
G = gravitational constant
r = Distant between two mass
Given M= 1unit and r = 1 unit, we get
F = G  {tex}\frac{1\times1}{1^2}{/tex}
⇒ F = G

questions.question: The weight of an object at the center of the earth of radius R is

options.option: {tex}\frac1{R^2}\;{/tex} times the weight at surface of the earth
R times the weight at the surface of the earth

questions.explanation:
The weight of the object is zero
Because weight = mg
And r = 0 so g = zero at the center of earth
Then w = zero

questions.question: An object weights 10 N in air. When immersed fully in water, it weighs only 8 N. The weight of the liquid displaced by the object will be
questions.explanation:
Weighs of air = 10 N
When immersed fully in water = 8N
The weight displaced = 10N – 8N = 2N

questions.question: A girl stands on a box having 60 cm length, 40 cm breadth and 20 cm width in three ways. In which of the following cases, pressure exerted by the brick will be

options.option: maximum when length and breadth from the base
maximum when width and length from the base
the same in all the above three cases
maximum when breadth and width from the base

questions.explanation: Now, according to question, when base is formed by breadth and width. Area will be minimum. And so, pressure will be maximum.

questions.question: School bags have a broader base to reduce
questions.explanation:
pressure = {tex}\frac{force}{area}{/tex}
So, if the area is more pressure exerted will be less and vice versa. This is the reason why the shoulder strap in school bags are broad. This helps in reducing the pressure exerted on the shoulders of the person and thus makes it more comfortable to carry around.

questions.question: Variation of acceleration due to gravity g with distance r from the centre of earth with (r > Re) is best given as
questions.explanation:
g =  {tex}\frac{GM}{r^2}{/tex}
So, g {tex}\propto{/tex} M and g {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac1{r^2}{/tex}

questions.question: There is no atmosphere on moon as

options.option: the gases have less requirement of velocity or energy to escape from its surface.

questions.explanation: Escape velocity is the speed that an object needs to be traveling to break free of a planet or moon's gravity well and leave it without further propulsion.

questions.question: Acceleration due to gravity is

options.option: Dependent directly on the radius of the planets under comparison are made of same material.
Inversely proportional to square of radius for all points on the surface of the planet.
Dependent on mass of the planet for points outside the planet.

questions.explanation:
The gravitational force exerted by any planet is based on the effective mass of the planet, which is in turn dependent on if you are on the surface of the planet, above the surface or below the surface of the planet.
F = {tex}\frac{Gm_1m_2}{r^2}{/tex}

questions.question: In a spring balance the space between 0 and 25 g marks is divided into 10 equal parts. The least count of spring balance is:
questions.explanation: L.C. = {tex}\frac{25-0}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}{10}{/tex} = 2.5 g wt

questions.question: The amount of heat energy lost by hot body in a given time depend on:
questions.explanation: The amount of heat energy lost by the hot body in a given time depends on temperature, surface nature and the area affect the loss.

questions.question: While performing an experiment a solid was immersed fully in water and it was observed that the solid has suffered an apparent loss in its weight. This apparent loss in weight of the solid must be equal to the:

options.option: weight of water displaced by the solid
weight of water filled in the vessel

questions.explanation: Weight loss = upthrust. So, app. weight = Wair - Wlost

questions.question: If we want to determine the volume of a solid by immersing it in water, the solid should be

options.option: heavier than water and insoluble in it

questions.explanation: It should be heavier than water and insoluble in it, so that it sinks and displaces the water and also it does not mix in the water.

questions.question: If the volume of a body is V and half of it is immersed in the liquid, the upthrust will be proportional to:

options.option: Difference in density of the body and half the density of the liquid.

questions.explanation:
Weight = W - U
{tex}V\rho g\;-\frac V2\sigma g\;=\;Vg\;\left(\rho\;-\frac\sigma2\;\right){/tex}

questions.question: A given solid is weighted in air using a spring balance. It is then weighed separately by immersing it fully first in vessel containing tap water and then in a vessel containing salt solution. The reading of the spring balance would be:
questions.explanation: The buoyant force acting on the solid depends on the factors such as the density of fluid and volume of solid. As the density of saltwater is highest among the given options, the buoyant force is maximum in the case of saltwater and the reading of spring balance would be least in this case.

questions.question: The weight of a body felt in tap water and salty water are WA and WB respectively, then :
questions.explanation: As density of salty water is more than the density of tap water. It will exert a greater buoyant force (upthrust) and, So, WA is greater than WB.

questions.question: Upthrust depends on:

options.option: both density of the liquid and dipped volume of the body immersed.

questions.explanation:
Factors affecting the Upthrust are:

  1. Volume of object immersed in liquid - as volume increases the upward force increases.
  2. Density of liquid: As the density of liquid increases the upward force exerted also increases.

questions.question: A student lowers a body in a liquid filled in a container. He finds that there is a maximum apparent loss in weight of the body when:

options.option: it is partially immersed and also touches the sides of the container.
it just touches the surface of the liquid.
it is partially immersed in the liquid.
it is completely immersed in the liquid.

questions.explanation: When a solid is completely immersed in the liquid, there is a maximum apparent loss in its weight due to the maximum volume of liquid displaced.

questions.question: The mass of body is 10 kg at a place where g = 10 m/s2, its weight is
questions.explanation: Weight = Mg = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 100 N

questions.question: The density of tap water is less than that of sea water. If an object is immersed completely in both one by one, its loss in weight will be :

options.option: Zero in sea water but not so in tap water.

questions.explanation: Loss in weight depends on upthrust which is dependent on the density of the liquid besides the volume immersed. Upthrust is more in seawater and so the loss in weight is more in it.

questions.question: The physical quantity in which apparent loss is observed when a solid object is immersed in water.
questions.explanation: Loss of weight is equal to the upthrust.

questions.question: A wooden block is floating in water contained in a beaker. During the free fall of the beaker, the upthrust on the block will be

options.option: Slightly less than the weight of the block.
Slightly more than the weight of the block.
Equal to the weight of the block

questions.explanation: The upthrust and the weight will be equal.

questions.question: Two particles are placed at some distance. If the mass of each of the two particles is doubled, keeping the distance between them unchanged, the value of gravitational force between them will be
questions.explanation:
The force of gravitational attraction between the two bodies of mass (M) and  (m) is 
  {tex}F=G \frac{M m}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Now,  If masses of both objects are halved without changing distance between them or M = 2M and 2m  
 {tex}F=G \frac{2 M \times 2 m}{r^{2}}=4 G \frac{M m}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Thus, force becomes four times as we increase the masses to double without altering the distances.

questions.question: At a place, value of g is less by 1% than its value on the surface of the Earth (Radius of Earth = 6400 km). The place is

options.option: 64 km above the surface of the Earth
32 km below the surface of the Earth
64 km below the surface of the Earth
30 km above the surface of the Earth

questions.explanation:
Let g be the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of the Earth.
At a certain place, acceleration due to gravity becomes,
g' = g - 1% of {tex} g=g-\frac{g}{100}{/tex} = 0.99 g
At height h, acceleration due to gravity becomes,
{tex}g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0.99 g=g\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R}\right){/tex}
{tex}h=\frac{0.01 \times R}{2}=\frac{0.01 \times 6400}{2} \mathrm{~km}{/tex} = 32 km
At depth d, acceleration due to gravity is given by
{tex}g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right) {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 0.99 g=g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right){/tex}
d = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} R = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 6400 km = 64 km

questions.question: A body is suspended from a spring balance kept in a satellite. The reading of the spring balance is W1 when the satellite goes in an orbit of radius R and W2 when it goes in an orbit of radius 2R. Which of the following is correct?
questions.explanation:
As the spring balance is kept in a satellite, it experiences weightlessness.
So, W1 = W2 = 0
Even if satellite changes its orbit, weight will not change.

questions.question: The height at which the acceleration due to gravity becomes {tex}\frac {g}{9}{/tex} in terms of R, where g is the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the Earth is (R is the radius of the Earth)
questions.explanation:
We know,
{tex}g=\frac{G M}{R^2}{/tex} ...(i)
Also {tex}g^{\prime}=\frac{G M}{(R+h)^2}{/tex} ...(ii)
Equating eqns. (i) and (ii), we get
9 R2 = (R + H)2
or 3R = R + h or h = 3R - R = 2R

questions.question: If a planet existed whose mass was twice that of Earth and whose radius 3 times greater, how much will a 1 kg mass weigh on the planet?
questions.explanation:
Here, Mp = 2 Me and Rp = 3Re
m = 1 kg, Wp = ?
As {tex}g_e=\frac{G M_e}{R_e^2} {/tex} and {tex}g_p=\frac{G M_p}{R_p^2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{g_p}{g_e}=\frac{M_p}{R_p^2} \times \frac{R_e^2}{M_e}{/tex}
or{tex}g_p=g_e \times \frac{M_p}{M_e} \times\left(\frac{R_e}{R_p}\right)^2{/tex}
or{tex}g_p=9.8 \times 2 \times\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2{/tex} = 2.17 m/s2
Wp = mgp = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.17 = 2.17 N

questions.question:
Match the column I with column II and select the correct option from the given codes.

Column I Column II
(a) g at height h (i) {tex}g\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R}\right){/tex}
(b) g at depth h (ii) {tex}\frac{G M}{R^2}{/tex}
(c) gpoles (iii) {tex}g\left(1-\frac{h}{R}\right){/tex}
(d) gsurface (iv) Maximum
questions.explanation:
Column I Column II
(a) g at height h (i) {tex}g\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R}\right){/tex}
(b) g at depth h (ii){tex}g\left(1-\frac{h}{R}\right){/tex} 
(c) gpoles (iii) Maximum
(d) gsurface (iv){tex}\frac{G M}{R^2}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following is not correct?

  1. If we drop a 10 lb iron ball and 1 lb aluminium ball from the top of a high platform inside a mine, then both will reach the floor at the same time.
  2. The gravitational force of attraction between the two bodies increases when their masses are increased and distance is reduced.
  3. We do not observe small objects moving towards each other because frictional force balances the gravitational pull.
  4. When a body is thrown up, the acceleration due to gravity at the topmost point is zero because velocity of the body is zero.
questions.explanation: The acceleration due to gravity is always directed downwards towards the centre of the Earth for a freely falling body.

questions.question: Priya buys a bag of sugar of weight W at a place on the equator. She takes it to Antarctica. The weight of a sugar bag at Antarctica

options.option: Remains same as the weight at a place on equator

questions.explanation:
At Antarctica, the value of acceleration due to gravity g is more, therefore, the weight of sugar bag will increase. 

We know at the equator, acceleration due to gravity is minimum and at the pole (Antarctica is at the South Pole), it is maximum. Therefore, the weight of the bag increases in Antarctica.

questions.question: Which of the following graphs shows an approximate variation of g with height h above the Earth’s surface?
questions.explanation: Value of g decreases with height.

questions.question:
Statement 1: A pendulum can vibrate in an artificial satellite.
Statement 2: Inside the satellite, acceleration due to gravity is zero.

options.option: Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false but statement 2 is true.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation:
Inside the satellite, g = 0
{tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{g}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{0}}=\infty{/tex}
Thus, the pendulum will not oscillate.

questions.question: A coil of wire with cross-sectional area 0.50 mm2 weighs 75N in air and 65N in water. The length of the coil in cm is (Take g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Area ={tex}\pi r^2{/tex}= 0.50 mm2 = 0.5 x 10-6 m2
Let L be its length.
So volume, V = 0.5 x 10-6Lm3
Weight in air, Wa= 75 N
Weight in water, WW = 65 N
So buoyant force by water = Wa - WW = 10 N
= V {tex}\times{/tex} density of water {tex}\times{/tex} g
or 10 = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6L {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10
{tex}L=\frac{10}{0.5 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{10 \times 10^2}{0.5}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{10^4}{5} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{10^6}{5} \mathrm{~cm}{/tex}

questions.question: A ball is released from the top of a tower of height h metres. It takes T seconds to reach the ground. What is the position of ball above the ground in {tex}\frac {T}{5}{/tex} seconds?
questions.explanation:
Here, height = h m, time ={tex}\frac{T}{5} \mathrm{~s}{/tex}
As s = {tex}u t+\frac{1}{2} a t^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore h^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} g\left(\frac{T}{5}\right)^2{/tex} or {tex} h^{\prime}=\frac{1}{25} h{/tex}
Position of the ball above the ground
= h - h' = {tex}h-\frac{h}{25}=\frac{24 h}{25} \mathrm{~m}{/tex}

questions.question: The gravitational force between two masses kept at a certain distance is p N. The same two masses are now kept in water and the distance between them is kept same. The gravitational force between these two masses in water is q N. Then
questions.explanation: Gravitational force is same in both the conditions because gravitational force between two objects is independent of the surrounding medium.

questions.question:
Consider the following figures 1 and 2. In figure 1, the mass of the body A in air is 0.2 kg. The mass of the water container (with water in it) is 0.5 kg. In figure 2, when A is completely immersed in the water of the container B, the readings of the respective balances change as shown.

What is the value of X?
questions.explanation:
Initially, weight of A = 0.2 kg wt
Weight of B = 0.5 kg wt
After complete immersion of A in water,
Weight of A = 0.15 kg wt
Reduction in weight of A = 0.2 - 0.15 = 0.05 kg wt
So, buoyant force applied on A by water = 0.05 kg wt
Required weight = weight of B + buoyant force on A = 0.5 + 0.05 = 0.55 kg wt
The reading of X = 0.55 kg

questions.question: Suppose, the acceleration due to gravity at the Earth’s surface is 10 m s-2and at the surface of Mars is 4 m s-2. A 60 kg passenger goes from the Earth to the Mars in a spaceship moving with a constant velocity. Neglect all other objects in the sky. Which part of figure best represents the weight (net gravitational force) of the passenger as a function of time?
questions.explanation:
Weight of passenger on Earth = m {tex}\times{/tex} ge = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 600 N
Weight of passenger on Mars = m {tex}\times{/tex} gm = 60 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 = 240 N
As the passenger is going from the Earth to the Mars in a spaceship moving with a constant velocity so the acceleration due to gravity because of Earth and Mars becomes negligible (zero) at some point in space. Thus, weight of passenger becomes zero.
After sometime, as he reaches near to the Mars, his weight increases because of acceleration due to gravity of Mars. Finally, at the surface of Mars, his weight becomes 240 N.

questions.question: A tank 2 m high is half filled with water and then filled to the top with oil of density 0.60 g/cc. What is the pressure at the bottom of the tank due to these liquids? (Take g = 10 m s-2)
questions.explanation:
Density of oil {tex}\left(\rho_0\right){/tex} = 0.60 g/cc = 600 kg/m3
Density of water{tex}\left(\rho_w\right){/tex} = 1 g/cm3 = 1000 kg/m3
Pressure due to water ={tex}h \rho_{\mathrm{w}} g{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 10000 N m-2
Pressure due to oil = {tex}h \rho_0 g{/tex} = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 6000 N m-2
Total pressure = 10000 + 6000 = 16000 N m-2 = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 N m-2

questions.question: An object floats in water such that {tex}\frac{1}{4} { th }{/tex} its volume is above the surface of water. When the same object is made to float in a sample of impure alcohol, that volume that remains outside the liquid is {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex} total volume. The relative density of the sample of alcohol will be
questions.explanation:
According to Archimedes’s principle, buoyant force, FB = Volume of the object immersed in liquid x density of liquid x acceleration due to gravity Let V be the volume of object.
For water: {tex}F_B=\frac{3 V}{4} \times \rho_w \times g=\frac{15}{2} V \rho_w{/tex} ...(i)
For alcohol: {tex}F_B=\frac{9 \mathrm{~V}}{10} \times \rho_a \times g=9 \mathrm{~V} \rho_a{/tex} ...(ii)
From eqn. (i) and (ii),
{tex}\frac{15}{2} V \rho_w=9 V \rho_a {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_w}=\frac{15}{2 \times 9}=\frac{15}{18}=0.83{/tex}

questions.question:
Statement 1: The weight of the body becomes zero at the centre of the earth.
Statement 2: As the distance from centre of earth decreases, acceleration due to gravity also decreases.

options.option: Both statement 1 and statement 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statement 1 and statement 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false

questions.explanation:
Variation of g with depth from surface of earth is given by {tex}g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right){/tex}
At the centre of earth, d = R, {tex}\therefore g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{R}{R}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Apparent weight of body = mg' = 0

questions.question: S.I. unit of power is ________.
questions.explanation: Power is measured in energy (joules) divided by time. The SI unit of power is the watt (W) or joule per second (J/s). Power is a scalar quantity, it has no direction.

questions.question: If the body starts from rest, then change in its kinetic energy is:

options.option: May be Positive or negative depending upon the mass of the body

questions.explanation:
Work = {tex}\;\frac12{/tex} m(v2 - u2)
If the object starts moving from the state of rest, therefore, initial velocity (u) will be equal to zero.
Therefore, equation (iii) can be written as
W = {tex}\;\frac12{/tex}m (v2 - 02)
W = {tex}\;\frac12{/tex}mv2
W = Positive kinetic energy

questions.question: Which of the following quantities have the same units?
questions.explanation:
The SI unit of work is joule and is denoted as ‘J’, which is named after an English physicist James Prescott Joule.
The 1 joule of work done is equal to 1N x 1 m.  Or, 1 joule = Nm
Energy is capable of doing work, therefore, the SI unit of energy is the same as of work.
Thus, the SI unit of energy is joule and is denoted by ‘J’.  A larger unit of energy is kilojoule and is denoted by kJ.

questions.question: If a body is stored at a height h then it will possess:
questions.explanation: The energy possessed by an object because of its position is called potential energy. For example; when a stone is kept at a height, it possesses some energy because of its height. Because of this potential energy, the object kept at a height falls over the ground.

questions.question: If the force applied on the body distances the body in the opposite direction of force applied then work done is:
questions.explanation:
When force is applied in the direction of displacement, the work done is considered as positive.
i.e. W = F {tex}\times{/tex} s
When force is applied in the opposite direction of displacement, the work done is considered negative.
i.e. W = -F {tex}\times{/tex} s = -Fs

questions.question: If the force is applied at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} then work done is:

options.option: W = FS Cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and F = Force
W = FS Sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and W = work
W = FS Tan {tex}\theta{/tex} and W = work

questions.explanation:
Work is defined as a force acting upon an object to cause a displacement
Mathematically, work can be expressed by the following equation.
W = F {tex}\times{/tex} S cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
Where W is work is done, S = displacement and F = Force

questions.question: What is the work done in lifting a body of mass 5 Kg vertically through 9 m? (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
M = 5 kg, h = 9 m
W = F {tex}\times{/tex} S  = m {tex}\times{/tex} g {tex}\times{/tex} h 
= 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 
= 450 J

questions.question: How are Joule (J) and ergs (erg) related?
questions.explanation:
Joule is the SI (mks) unit of the energy while the erg is the CGS unit of energy.
1 Joule of Energy = 1 newton {tex}\times{/tex} 1 meter.
1 erg = 1dyne {tex}\times{/tex} 1cm.
1 Joule = 1 newton {tex}\times{/tex} 1 meter =105 dyne {tex}\times{/tex} 102 cm = 107 erg.

questions.question: A piece of wood is held underwater. The upthrust on it is:

options.option: less than the weight of the wood
equal to the weight of the wood

questions.explanation: The density of wood is less than the density of water. That is why it floats on the surface of the water and if immersed, the upthrust acting on the wooden piece will be greater than its weight pulling it downwards, so it comes to the surface.

questions.question: A balloon filled with hydrogen rises upwards because:

options.option: the weight of the balloon is less than the weight of the air displaced by it.
the pressure inside the balloon is greater than the pressure outside

questions.explanation: As the weight of the balloon is less than the weight of air displaced by it therefore the balloon rises in the air. In balloons, hydrogen is normally filled because it is lighter than air.

questions.question: Archimedes principle states that when a body is totally or partially immersed in a fluid the upthrust is equal to:

options.option: the weight of the fluid displaced by it

questions.explanation:
Archimedes principle states that when a body is partly or totally immersed in a fluid there is an upthrust that is equal to the weight of fluid displaced.
Upthrust = apparent loss of weight of object = weight in air - weight in liquid.

questions.question: The stem of a hydrometer is made narrow because it:
questions.explanation: A hydrometer is an instrument used for determining the density of a liquid. It usually consists of a glass float with a long thin stem that is graduated. The glass float is a large hollow bulb which increases the buoyancy so that the hydrometer floats. The narrow stem increases the sensitivity of the hydrometer. The bottom of the hydrometer is made heavier by loading it with lead shots so that it floats vertically.

questions.question: If extra cargo is loaded on the ship, the ship will:

options.option: tend to turn over and float bottom upwards

questions.explanation: On loading extra cargo, the weight of the ship increases. So, the ship sinks further till the weight of water displaced by it is again equal to the weight of the loaded ship. Conversely, if the ship is unloaded, it rises up.

questions.question: The upward force experienced by a body immersed partially or fully in a fluid is called ________.
questions.explanation: A body that is completely or partially submerged in fluid experiences an upward force called the Buoyant Force, which is equal in magnitude to the weight of the fluid displaced by the object. The buoyant force depends on the mass of the object and is independent of the density of the solid.

questions.question: The potential energy of a person is minimum when:

options.option: a person is sitting in a chair
a person is lying on the ground
a person is sitting on the ground

questions.explanation: The potential energy of your body is least when you are lying down on the ground. An object can store energy as a result of its position. The stored energy of position is called potential energy.

questions.question: No work is done when:

options.option: textbook is lowered diagonally at a constant velocity
a box is pushed along horizontal floor
a nail is hammered into a wooden box
there is no component of force, parallel to the direction of motion

questions.explanation: Work is done whenever a force or a component of a force results in a displacement. No component of the force is acting in the direction of motion.

questions.question: When the speed of a moving object is doubled, its:
questions.explanation:
K.E. = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2
If speed double so, v = 2v
K.E. = {tex}\frac12{/tex} m(2v)2 ={tex}\frac12{/tex} m(4v2) = 4({tex}\frac12{/tex}mv2)

questions.question: When a stone of mass 'm' falls through a vertical height 'd', the decrease in the gravitational energy is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of the stone = m
Vertical distance, i.e. height h = d
Potential energy = mgh = mgd

questions.question: A 1 kg mass has a kinetic energy of 1 joule when its speed is:
questions.explanation:
K.E = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2
We'll replace K.E i.e kinectic energy by 1 J and mass (m) by 1
1 = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} v2
2 = v2
{tex}\sqrt2{/tex} = v
 v = 1.4 ms-1

questions.question: An iron sphere of mass 30 kg has the same diameter as an aluminum sphere whose mass is 10.5 kg. The spheres are dropped simultaneously from a cliff. When they are 10 m from the ground, they have the same.
questions.explanation: When both spheres are dropped simultaneously from a cliff, they have the same acceleration. Because during free-fall acceleration of the body becomes equals to (g= 9.8m/s2)  and 'g' depends on the mass of earth and radius of the earth.

questions.question: When a body is whirled in a circle the work done by the centripetal force is:
questions.explanation: When a body is whirled in a circle the work done by the centripetal force is zero Because work is done is defined as the distance an object is moved in the direction of the force applied and since the centripetal force acts perpendicular to the velocity (direction of motion) the work done is 0. Work done by the centripetal force is zero only when the radial position does not change.

questions.question: An object of mass 1 kg has a potential energy of 1 joule relative to the ground when it is at a height of:
questions.explanation:
P.E. = mgh
m= 1 kg P.E. = 1J g = 9.8m/s2
1 = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} h
h = {tex}1 \over 9.8{/tex} = 0.102 m

questions.question: An object of mass 5 kg falls from a height of 5 m above the ground. The loss of potential energy of the mass is:
questions.explanation:
P.E. = mgh
m = 5kg h = 5m  g = 9.8m/s2
P.E. = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
P.E. = 25 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8
P.E. = 245 J

questions.question: Weight lifter lifts 240 kg from the ground to a height of 2.5 m in 3 seconds. His average power is:
questions.explanation:
m = 240 kg ,g = 9.8 m/s2
Force = weight = mg = 240(9.8) = 2352  N
displacment = 2.5m and t = 3 second
Power = {tex}\frac{force\times\;displacement}{time}\;{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{2352\;\times\;2.5}3{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{5880}3{/tex} = 1960W.

questions.question: A boy throws a ball vertically upwards. As the ball rises its total energy (neglect friction):
questions.explanation: The kinetic energy changes into the potential energy when stone rises up. The potential energy then changes back to the kinetic energy, when a stone falls down. A boy throws a ball vertically upwards. As the ball rises its total energy remains the same.

questions.question: When a force retards the motion of a body the work done is:
questions.explanation:
The nature of work done in case of retarding motion is negative.
Suppose a force F brings a body moving with velocity v to rest (retards), then work done = change in KE = KEf = KEi
W = 0 - {tex}\frac12{/tex} mu2 = -{tex}\frac12{/tex}mu2

questions.question: ________ is the SI unit of work.
questions.explanation:
The SI unit of work is joule and is denoted as ‘J’, which is named after an English physicist James Prescott Joule.
The 1 joule of work done is equal to 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m. or, 1 joule = Nm.

questions.question: The rate of doing work is called ________.
questions.explanation: Power is defined as the rate of doing work or the rate of transfer of energy.

questions.question: A body at rest may have:
questions.explanation: Potential energy is the energy of a body due to its height, hence any object that is not at the center of the earth is under a gravitational pull and has an energy that can enable it to travel if unhindered towards the center. Hence all the objects on earth that are at rest, have gravitational potential energy.

questions.question: The work done by a force on a body will be positive if the:

options.option: body moves opposite to the direction of the applied force
body moves perpendicular to the direction of motion
the body moves along the direction of the applied force

questions.explanation: The work done is positive if the applied force is in the same direction as the direction of motion.

questions.question: The cgs. unit of work is:
questions.explanation: The unit of work in the cgs system is dyne cm or erg.

questions.question: One horsepower is equal to:
questions.explanation:
1  HP = 550 Foot Pound per second.
1 Pound = 4.44822 meters.
1 foot is 0.3408 meters.
1 Hp = 550 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.44822 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.3048 meters
= 745.69 Nm/s = 746 Watts.

questions.question: One joule work is said to be done when:

options.option: a force of 1 N displaces a body by 1 cm
a force of 1 dyne displaces a body by 1 m
a force of 1 N displaces a body by 1 m
a force of 1 dyne displaces a body by 1 cm

questions.explanation:
1 joule(J) is the amount of work done when a force of 1 Newton(N) displaces an object 1 meter(m).
1J = 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1m or 1J = 1Nm.

questions.question: The SI unit of energy is ________.
questions.explanation: SI unit of energy is the same as of work. The SI unit of energy is joule and is denoted by ‘J’.

questions.question: Kilowatt hour is a unit of ________.
questions.explanation: Commercial unit of energy is kilowatt hour (kWh).

questions.question: The potential energy of a body is by virtue of its ________.
questions.explanation: The energy possessed by an object because of its position is called potential energy.

questions.question: A mass 'm' falls from a height 'h'. At any point on its path the total energy is:
questions.explanation: When a stone is dropped from a height H, it has Potential Energy (PE) equal to mgh and Kinetic Energy (KKE) is 0. When it falls, according. to the law of conservation of energy, its Total Energy (PE + KE) will always remain constant i.e mgh.

questions.question: If 60 W bulb and 40 W bulb are connected in parallel to a source of 220 V potential, the bulb glowing brighter is:
questions.explanation:
In parallel, 60W will glow brighter as
P = VI
P = {tex}\frac{V^2}R{/tex} ....(I ={tex}\frac{V^{}}R{/tex})
Therefore power is inversely proportional to R.
In 60W resistance is less. So it will glow brighter in parallel.

questions.question: If the bulbs of 60 W and 40 W are connected in series to a 220 V source the bulb which glows brighter is:

options.option: Depends on the make of the bulb

questions.explanation:
In series 40W bulb glow brighter as
P = VI (P – power)
P = I2R (V= IR)
Therefore power is directly proportional to resistance. The resistance of the 40W bulb is higher.
So, it will have more power and it will glow brighter.

questions.question: A mass m is moving in a circle of radius 1 m with a uniform speed of 5 m/s. The work done when its radius turns by 60o is:
questions.explanation: The object moves in a circular path. In this case, the centripetal force acts on the object at right angles to the direction of motion of an object. So, the work done by the object in a circular path is zero.

questions.question: A man holds and displaces a 20 kg mass horizontally in his stretched hand:

options.option: The work done against gravity cannot be estimated but non-zero.
The work done against gravity is equal to the muscular energy
He does 200 joules of work per metre
He does zero work against the force of gravity

questions.explanation: He does zero work against the force of gravity because no displacement is done.

questions.question: Two cars A and B approaching each other with a momentum of 50 kg m/s and 25 kg m/s respectively, stick to each other after the collision. The momentum after the collision is:
questions.explanation: The momentum after the collision is in the direction of A because of less momentum.

questions.question: In a collision:

options.option: the momentum of the system is constant

questions.explanation: The total momentum of the system just before the collision is the same as the total momentum just after the collision. So, the momentum of the system is constant.

questions.question: In the explosion of a cracker/bomb:
questions.explanation: When a cracker bursts, its pieces move out in all directions. We explain it with the help of the law of conservation of momentum. In this case, initially, the momentum of the cracker is zero. Therefore, when it bursts, its pieces move out in different directions with some specific momentum. However, if we add up the momentum of these various pieces, the sum of the momenta will be zero.

questions.question: Which of the following energy cannot be completely converted into other forms due to the transmission system?
questions.explanation: All forms of energy can be completely converted to heat, but heat cannot be completely converted into work or other forms of energy because of the entropy heat contains.

questions.question: In an oscillating simple pendulum:

options.option: Work is done by the force of gravity

questions.explanation: The pendulum swings down and the potential energy is converted to kinetic energy.  At the bottom of the swing, the potential energy is all gone (this is the lowest it goes) and it is all kinetic energy.  This is also the fastest that it goes.

questions.question: A microphone converts sound energy to:
questions.explanation: Microphones are loudspeakers in reverse, it converts sound (mechanical) energy to electrical energy.

questions.question: A battery lights a bulb. The sequence of energy transfer in the process is:

options.option: chemical energy to electrical energy and then to heat and light

questions.explanation: When a bulb is connected to a battery, then the chemical energy of the battery is transferred into electrical energy. When the bulb receives this electrical energy, then it converts it into light and heat energy.

questions.question: An elevator with load, weighting 500 kg is moving up with a velocity of 0.2 ms-1. The power of the motor in horsepower is: (1 H.P. = 746 watt)
questions.explanation:
Force = m g = 500 kg {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 m/s² = 4900 N
Velocity = 0.2 m/s
Now, power = Force {tex}\times{/tex} velocity = 4900 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 = 980 W
Now 1 H.P. = 746 watt
So, 980 W = {tex}980  \over 746{/tex} = 1.3 hp

questions.question: When a body falls freely towards the earth, then its total energy
questions.explanation: Since, total energy of the system is always conserved, so when a body falls freely towards the earth, then its total energy remains constant i.e., the sum of the potential energy and kinetic energy of the body would be same at all points.

questions.question: A car is accelerated on a leveled road and attains a velocity 4 times of its initial velocity. In this process the potential energy of the car

options.option: becomes twice to that of initial

questions.explanation: Potential energy will not change as the road is leveled and the height of the body remains the same, although its speed increases.

questions.question: In case of negative work the angle between the force and displacement is:
questions.explanation: When force is applied in opposite direction of displacement, the work done is considered as negative and the angle between the force and displacement is 180o.

questions.question: A girl is carrying a school bag of 3 kg mass on her back and moves 200 m on a leveled road. The work done against the gravitational force will be (g = 10m/s2)
questions.explanation: The work done is zero because between the road and girl the angle made is 90 so the value of cos 90 is zero so when we use formula w = f s cos90 the answer will zero.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not the unit of energy?
questions.explanation: Since energy is capacity of doing work, therefore, the SI unit of energy is same as of work.

questions.question: An electric bulb of 60 W burns for 5 hours a day. The cost of electricity involved in a month of 30 days at ₹ 3.00 per unit is (in ₹)
questions.explanation:
Power = {tex}Energy \ consumed \over Time{/tex}
Given power = 60W = 0.06kW
Therefore, For one-day energy consumed, 0.06kW = {tex}\frac{Energy\;consumed\;}{5\;hr}{/tex}
0.30kWh = Energy consumed
0.30 unit one day.
Now for 30 days, = 0.30  {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 9 UNIT
Given for 1 unit cost = ₹ 3
Hence cost for 9 unit = 9 × 3 = ₹ 27

questions.question: A tank contains water of mass 'm' to a height 'h'. The potential energy associated is

options.option: Depends on the radius of the tank

questions.explanation:
If you consider gravitational field to be constant till height 'h’ then we know that Center of the mass of tank lies {tex}\frac h2{/tex}height above ground level.
Now potential energy will be = m{tex} \times{/tex}g {tex} \times{/tex} ({tex}\frac{h}{2}{/tex}) .

questions.question: If a force of F newton moves a body with constant speed v, the power delivered by it is:
questions.explanation: Power = Force {tex}\times{/tex} velocity = F {tex}\times{/tex} v

questions.question: The value of one kWh is:
questions.explanation:
One kilowatt-hour is equal to 3600000 joules:
1kWh = 3600000J = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106J

questions.question: The work done on an object does not depend upon the
questions.explanation:
We know that, W = F.d cos θ
Here, F = force applied on the object, d = displacement and θ is angle between force and displacement. So, the work done on an object does not depend upon the initial velocity of the object.

questions.question: Water stored in a dam possess
questions.explanation: Water stored in a dam possesses potential energy. And once the water is falling it has kinetic energy.

questions.question: A body is falling from a height h. After it has fallen a height {tex}\frac h2{/tex} . it will possess
questions.explanation:
As we know that when a body is at height h, it has
total energy = KE + PE
and at height ‘h’, velocity of body is zero
SO, KE = 0 and PE = mgh
Now, at height {tex}\frac h2{/tex}
its PE = mg {tex}\frac h2{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac {mgh}2{/tex} .......... (i)
and KE = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} mv2
Where, velocity can be determined by equation of motion
v2 = u2 + 2gh
So, v2 = {tex}\;\frac {2gh}2{/tex} ⇒ v ={tex}\sqrt{gh}{/tex}
So, KE = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}m{tex}(\sqrt{gh)}^2{/tex} ............ (ii)
So, from equation (i) and (ii) body at ({tex}\frac h2{/tex}) posses half potential energy and half kinetic energy.

questions.question: If 1 newton of force displaces a body by 1 m, the work done is:
questions.explanation:
1 joule(J) is the amount of work done when a force of 1 Newton(N) displaces an object 1 meter(m).
1J = 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1m or 1J = 1Nm.

questions.question: On tripling the speed of motion of a body, the change in K.E. is:
questions.explanation:
K.E. is proportional to V2.
So, when speed is tripled, KE. is nine-time.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true for the pressure exerted by a solid body?

options.option: It does not depends upon the area of contact for the  magnitude of force
Greater the area over which the force acts, greater is the pressure
It does not depend upon the area of contact for the same magnitude of force
It depends upon the area of contact for the same magnitude of the force:

questions.explanation: {tex}P\;{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac FA{/tex} , For the same force {tex}P\;\propto\;\frac1A{/tex}

questions.question: To observe and compare the pressure exerted by solid iron cuboid, a student marked all the faces of cuboid from 1 to 6. After doing the experiment with all faces of cuboid, he observed that

options.option: all the faces of cuboid exerted same pressure on the sand
opposite parallel faces of the cuboid exerted some pressure on the sand
adjacent faces of cuboid exerted the same pressure
opposite parallel faces of cuboid exerted unequal pressure on the sand

questions.explanation: Since force per unit area remains the same, so the same conclusion is drawn by both the students.

questions.question: Two discs of the same radii and the same mass are resting over one another co-axially on the sand. If one disc is removed gently, then the pressure exerted by the single-disc will be:
questions.explanation: On removing the disc, weight reduces to half.

questions.question: A cylinder and a cone having radius r1 and r2 respectively (r1 > r2) are having the same mass. Then pressure exerted by the cylinder is:
questions.explanation:
{tex}P\;\propto\;\frac1A{/tex}
The pressure is inversely proportional to the area and ACylinder > ACone

questions.question: In doing the experiment, a student puts the solid iron cuboid in an upright position on loose sand. After that, he has laid it. In these two cases:

options.option: thrust is the same and its effects are also the same
thrust is same but effects are different
effects of the thrust are same but different thrusts are there
the effect of thrust on sand is smaller while in an upright position than lying.

questions.explanation: As the contact area changes, the effect of thrust changes.

questions.question: A solid iron cuboid is kept on a tabletop. The mass of cuboid is 6 kg and its dimensions are 30 cm × 15 cm× 10 cm. Force and pressure exerted by the cuboid on the tabletop if it made to lie on the tabletop with its side dimensions 30m {tex}\times{/tex} 15 cm is:
questions.explanation:
Thrust = Force× Weight of cuboid = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 = 58.8N
and pressure exerted = {tex}\;\frac{Thrust}{Base\;area}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{58.8}{0.30\;\times0.15}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{58.8}{0.045}{/tex} = 1306.66N.

questions.question: A total of 4900 J was spent in lifting a mass of 50 kg. the mass was raised to a height of:
questions.explanation:
P.E. = mgh
m= 50 kg P.E. = 4900J g = 9.8m/s2
4900 = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} h
4900 = 490 {tex}\times{/tex} h
h = {tex}4900 \over 490{/tex} = 10 m

questions.question:
One unit of electricity is consumed by A : P = 40 W bulb used for t = 25 hours
B : P = 20 W bulb used for t = 50 hours
Both A and B are true as energy E in kWh is best related as
questions.explanation:
Energy = Power {tex}\times{/tex} time
i.e. E = Watt {tex}\times{/tex} time
When we say we have consumed 1 unit of electricity, that means we have consumed 1kWhr of electrical energy.
1 Unit = kilo watt-hour.
i.e. 1unit = 1kWhr

questions.question: To observe and compare the pressure exerted by solid iron cuboid on the sand, a student takes a cuboid of 5 kg and has a dimension of its base 30 cm ×15 cm and measures its pressure, then he mounts the cuboid on a cylinder having a radius of base 7 cm and weight equal to 1 kg. The pressure of the whole set-up on the sand is:
questions.explanation:
P1 = {tex}\;\frac{F_1}{A_1}{/tex} {tex}=\;\frac{M_1g}{A_1}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{5\times\;9.8}{0.30\times0.15}{/tex} =1.09 {tex}\times{/tex} 103N/m2
P2 ={tex}\;\frac{F_2}{A_2}{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{(M_1\;+\;M_2)g}{\mathrm{{tex}\pi{/tex}r}^2}{/tex} ={tex}\frac{(5\;+1)\times\;9.8}{0.0154}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{58.8}{0.0154}\;{/tex} = 3.82 {tex}\times{/tex} 103N/m2
{tex}\;\frac{P2}{P_1}{/tex}= {tex}\;\frac{3.82\;\times10^3}{1.09\;\times10^3}{/tex} = 3.5

questions.question: A boy of mass 50 kg runs up a staircase of 45 steps in 9 s. If the height of each step is 15 cm, find his power. Take g = 10 ms–2.
questions.explanation:
Weight of the boy, mg = 50 kg × 10 m s–2 = 500 N
Height of the staircase, h = 45 × {tex}\frac{15}{100}{/tex} m = 6.75 m
Time taken to climb, t = 9 s
Power = {tex}\frac{Work\;done}{time\;taken}{/tex} 
P ={tex}\frac{mgh}t\;=\;\frac{500\;N\;\times\;6.75\;m\;}{\;9s}{/tex}  = 375 W.
Power is 375 W.

questions.question: Two masses m and 2m are dropped from a certain height 'h'. Then on reaching the ground,

options.option: K.E. of the heavier is more than that of the lighter.
K.E. of the heavier is 4 times the K.E. of the lighter
K.E. of the lighter is 4 times the K.E. of the heavier

questions.explanation:
P.E. of object1 = mgh
P.E. of object 2 = 2mgh
On reaching the ground the P.E. of both the objects gets completely transformed into K.E.
Therefore K.E. of object 2 > K.E. of object 1.

questions.question: Calculate the potential energy of a person having 60 kg mass on the summit of Mt. Everest. Height of Mt. Everest is 8848 m from sea level. (g = 9.8 m/s2).
questions.explanation:
Mass m = 60 kg
height h = 8848 m
g = 9.8 m/s2
Potential energy U = mgh= (60 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 8848) = 52,02,624 J

questions.question: A horse exerts a force of 200N to pull the cart. If the horse-cart system moves with velocity 36km/h on the level road., then find the power of the horse in terms of horsepower (1hp = 746W).
questions.explanation:
Velocity = 36km/h  = 10m/s
w = f {tex}\times{/tex} s = 2000 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 2000j
P = {tex}\frac{W}t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2000}1{/tex} = 2000 w
746W = 1hp
So, 2000 w = {tex}\frac{2000}{746}{/tex} = 2.68 h.p

questions.question: If an electric iron 1200W is used for 30 minutes every day, then electric energy consumed in the month of April is:
questions.explanation:
P = {tex}\frac{1200}{1000}{/tex} = 1.2 kW
t = {tex}\frac{30}{60}{/tex} = 0.5 h
E = Power {tex}\times{/tex} time {tex}\times{/tex} days = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 18 kWh.

questions.question: The heart does 1.5 J of work in each heartbeat. How many times per minute does it beat if its power is 2watt?
questions.explanation:
Total work = p {tex}\times{/tex} t = 120 J ,
Number times heartbeat in 1 min. = total work done / work done in each beat
   = {tex}\frac{120}{1.5}{/tex} = 80 times

questions.question: A ball dropped from a height of 10m. if the energy of the ball reduces by 40% after striking the ground, how much high can the bounce back? (g = 10m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Energy = mgh =  m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 100m Joule.
Energy is reduced by 40% then the remaining energy is 60m Joule.
Therefore 60 m = m {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} h`
or h` = 6 m

questions.question: A machine does 192 J of work in 24 Sec. What is the power of the machine?
questions.explanation: P = {tex}\frac Wt\;{/tex} = {tex}\;\frac{192J}{24\;sec}{/tex} = 8 W

questions.question: A rickshaw puller pulls the rickshaw by applying a force of 100 N. If the rickshaw moves with a constant velocity of 36 km/h. Find the power of rickshaw puller.
questions.explanation:
Force = 100 N
Velocity = 36 k m/h = 36 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}5 \over 18{/tex}= 10 m/s
Power = Force {tex}\times{/tex} Velocity Power = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 10
Power = 1000 Watt
Therefore the power of rickshaw puller = 1000 Watt

questions.question: A boy of mass 50 kg runs up a flight of 120 stairs, each measuring a height of 25 cm, in one minute and 40 seconds. If g = 10 m s-2, the power of the boy is
questions.explanation:
Here, mass, m = 50 kg
Height of the staircase, h = {tex}120 \times \frac{25}{100}=30 \mathrm{~m}{/tex}
Time taken to climb =1 min 40 s = 100 s
We know, Power, P {tex}=\frac{\text { Work done }}{\text { Time taken }}=\frac{m g h}{t}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{50 \times 10 \times 30}{100}=150 \mathrm{~W}{/tex}

questions.question: A truck of mass 800 kg generates a power of 20000 W. How much time does the truck need to accelerate from a speed of 20 m s-1 to 30 m s-1?
questions.explanation:
Due to conservation of energy,
input energy = output energy
Power {tex}\times{/tex} time = increase in kinetic energy
{tex}P \times t=\frac{1}{2}\left[m v^2-m u^2\right]{/tex}
(20000)(t) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[(800)(30)^2-(800)(20)^2\right] {/tex} or t = 10 s

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is thrown up vertically with a kinetic energy of 490 J. If the acceleration due to gravity is 9.8 m s-2, the height at which the kinetic energy of the body becomes half of the original value is
questions.explanation:
We know, if a body is thrown up vertically its KE goes on decreasing and PE goes on increasing. There is conversion of KE into PE. When kinetic energy becomes half of original value, the other half is converted into potential energy.
Therefore, {tex}\frac{490}{2}=2 \times 9.8 \times h{/tex}
or h = {tex}\frac{490}{2 \times 2 \times 9.8}{/tex} or h = 12.5
Thus, the required height is 12.5 m.

questions.question:
Statement 1: If a light body and a heavy body possess the same momentum, the lighter body will possess more kinetic energy.
Statement 2: The kinetic energy of a body varies as the square of its velocity.

options.option: Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.

questions.explanation:
Relation between momentum and KE (EK) is
{tex}p=\sqrt{2 m E_k}{/tex}
If mass of body decreases, then KE should increase accordingly to maintain constant momentum.
Also, {tex}E_K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex} or {tex}E_K \propto v^2{/tex}

questions.question: Coolie A takes 1 min to raise a box of mass m through a height of 2.0 m. Another coolie B takes 30 s for the same job. Which one has greater power?
questions.explanation:
Power = {tex}\frac{\text { Work }}{\text { time }}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done by both of them is same.
Hence, coolie who have taken less time has greater power i.e., coolie B.

questions.question:
Statement 1: A man carrying a bucket of water and walking on a rough level road with a uniform velocity does no work while carrying the bucket.
Statement 2: The work done on a body by a force (F) in giving it a displacement (S) is defined as W{tex}=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=F S \cos \theta, {/tex} where {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between vectors {tex}\vec{F} {/tex} and {tex}\vec{S}{/tex}.

options.option: Both statements 1 and 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statements 1 and 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false.

questions.explanation:
While carrying a bucket, angle between vectors F and S becomes 90°.
So, {tex}W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{S}=F S \cos \theta{/tex} = FS cos 90o = 0
Hence, work done is zero.

questions.question:
Match the columns and select the correct option from the codes given here.

Column I Column II
(a) Work done by all the forces (i) Change in potential energy
(b) Work done by conservative forces (ii) Change in kinetic energy
(c) Work done by external forces (iii) Change in mechanical energy
(d) Work done in uniform velocity (iv) Zero
Water falls from a height of 30 m at the rate of 20 kg/s to operate a turbine. The losses due to frictional forces are 20% of energy. How much power is generated by the turbine? (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Mass of water falling/second = 20 kg/s h = 30 m, g = 10 m/s2, loss = 20% i.e., 80% is used.
Power generated = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 = 4800 W = 4.8 kW

questions.question: A truck and a car having equal kinetic energies are stopped by applying equal retarding force. If S1 and S2 are the distances covered by truck and car respectively, before stopping, then
questions.explanation:
Let m1, m2 be the masses and u1, u2 be the initial velocities of the truck and car respectively.
As KE of both are equal.
So, {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_1 u_1^2=\frac{1}{2} m_2 u_2^2 {/tex} or {tex}\frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{u_2^2}{u_1^2}{/tex}
As equal retarding force is applied on both then
{tex}0=u_1^2-\frac{2 f S_1}{m_1} \Rightarrow S_1=\frac{m_1 u_1^2}{2 f}{/tex}
and {tex}0=u_2^2-\frac{2 f S_2}{m_2} \Rightarrow S_2=\frac{m_2 u_2^2}{2 f}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{S_1}{S_2}=\frac{m_1}{m_2} \frac{u_1^2}{u_2^2} {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{S_1}{S_2}=\frac{1}{1} {/tex}  or S1 = S2

questions.question: A box of 5 kg mass moves up a ramp from point A to point Bat a constant speed. Given that the surfaces are smooth (frictionless). What is the gain in the potential energy by the box? (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Gravitational potential energy
E = mgh = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 = 400 J

questions.question: A graph of the total energy of a freely falling body from a height is
questions.explanation: Total energy remains constant.

questions.question:
Statement 1: Potential energy of a stretched or compressed spring is directly proportional to square of extension or compression.
Statement 2: Graph between potential energy of a spring versus the extension or compression of the spring is a straight line.

options.option: Both statement 1 and statement 2 are true and statement 2 is the correct explanation of statement 1.
Both statement 1 and statement 2 are true but statement 2 is not the correct explanation of statement 1.
Statement 1 is true but statement 2 is false

questions.explanation:
Potential energy (U){tex}=\frac{1}{2} k x^2 {/tex} i.e. {tex} U \propto x^2{/tex}
This is the equation of parabola, so graph between U and x is a parabola, not straight line.

questions.question:
Potential energy of an object at a height h is Ep = mgh. This equation is true only if

  1. The acceleration due to gravity is constant.
  2. The potential energy is path dependent.
questions.explanation: Only (i) is true.

questions.question: A skier of mass 50 kg stands at point P at the top of the ski jump and moves from P to Q and takes off his jump at Q as shown in figure. If 50% of the change in the gravitational potential energy of the skier between points P and Q becomes the kinetic energy at Q, then the speed at which the skier arrives at Q is
questions.explanation:
Change in gravitational potential energy of the skier, EP = mgh = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 {tex}\times{/tex} [60 - 20]= 20000 J = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J
Kinetic energy, EK = 50% of EP {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}=1 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
{tex}E_K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \Rightarrow v=\sqrt{\frac{2 E_K}{m}}{/tex}{tex}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^4}{50}}=20 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}{/tex}

questions.question: A mass of M kg is suspended by a weightless string. The horizontal force that is required to displace it until the string making an angle of 45° with the initial vertical direction is
questions.explanation:
Work done in displacement is equal to gain in potential energy.

Work done ={tex}F \times I \sin 45^{\circ}=\frac{F I}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
Gain in potential energy
= Mg(l - lcos45°)
{tex}=M g I\left(1-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{F I}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{M g I(\sqrt{2}-1)}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}F=M g(\sqrt{2}-1){/tex}

questions.question: A body moves under a variable force. For the body a plot of velocity versus time is shown in figure. The correct statement is

options.option: In moving from O to A, work done by the force on the body is negative
In moving from A to B, the body does work on the system
In moving from C to D, work done by the force on the body is positive
In moving from B to C, work done by the force on the body is positive

questions.explanation: In moving from C to D, slope of graph is negative which means acceleration (force) is negative and displacement is also negative as the area under curve CD lies below the time axis. Hence, work done by the force on the body is positive.

questions.question: Two inclined frictionless tracks, one gradual and the other steep meet at point A from where two stones (I and II) are allowed to slide down from rest, one on each track as shown in figure. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.option: Both the stones reach the bottom at the same time but not with the same speed.
Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone II reaches the bottom earlier than stone I.
Both the stones reach the bottom at different times and with different speeds.
Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone I reaches the bottom earlier than stone II.

questions.explanation:
AB and AC are two smooth planes inclined to the horizontal at different angles.
As height of both the planes are same, therefore, both the stones will reach the bottom with same speed.
{tex}\because v=\sqrt{2 g h}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} v1 = v2

From figure, acceleration, {tex}a_1=g \sin \theta_1{/tex}
acceleration, a2 = g sin{tex}\theta_2{/tex}
As {tex}\theta_2{/tex} > {tex}\theta_1{/tex}, so a2 > a1
Using, v = u + at
v1 = u1 + a1t1 ; v1 = a1t1
t1 = {tex}\frac{v_1}{a_1}{/tex}
Similarly, t2 = {tex}\frac{v_2}{a_2} {/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} a2 > a1 So, t2 < t1
Hence, stone II takes less time to reach the bottom.

questions.question: The diagram shows a cyclist moving down a small hill at P before travelling on a flat road after point Q. The total mass of the cyclist and his bicycle is 60 kg and the speed of the cyclist at P is 10 m s-1. Which of the following is incorrect? (Take acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m s-2)

options.option: The loss of gravitational energy between P and Q is 1800 J.
The cyclist does not require a greater power when he travels at a higher constant speed.
The kinetic energy at P is 3000 J.

questions.explanation:
When he travels at a higher constant speed, so a higher rate of work is done against air resistance.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Higher power is required.

questions.question:
The diagram shows an object released from point X and travelling on a frictionless track. If a heavier object is taken in consideration then

  1. It will move with a greater speed at Y.
  2. The speed of the object at M is 10 m s-1.
questions.explanation:
The speed of the object is affected by the height from which it is released.
{tex}m g h=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 {/tex} or {tex}v=\sqrt{2 g h} {/tex} or {tex}v \propto \sqrt{h}{/tex}
So, heavier object will not move with a greater speed at Y.
Speed of the object at M:
Loss in PE = KE
mg(25 - 20) = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^2 {/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} v2 = 100 or v = 10 ms-1

questions.question: S.I unit of sound waves wavelength is:
